# Chapter 1: So This Mental Thing Happened

Right so I'm revising for Biology - and by revising I mean watching this TikTok about how some girl in Manchester found out her boyfriend was also dating her mum, proper Jeremy Kyle stuff - when I get this text at midnight. Three blood drop emojis. Just that. 🩸🩸🩸

No name, number's not saved. 

I screenshot it obviously, send it to Keya but she's not replying because she's probably round Jordan Fletcher's house "watching Netflix" which everyone knows means getting fingered on his nan's sofa while she's upstairs watching Corrie on catch-up. Rank.

I'm staring at these blood drops thinking what the fuck? Like is this:
a) Period emergency from some Year 9 who got my number wrong
b) Lewis finally snapped and murdered his stepdad (we've all been waiting)
c) Someone's taken up cutting and wants attention
d) Wrong number from some weirdo

My money's on period emergency because last week Yasmin gave my number to her little sister's mate who needed a pad during PE and somehow that turned into me being the unofficial Period Supplier for Year 9s who can't ask their mums. I charge them a quid each though so it's decent money.

Twenty minutes later my phone goes off again. Same number.

"sorry wrong person 💀"

Then immediately: "Please don't tell anyone"

Then: "Actually wait"

Then: "I'll explain at school"

Then: "Do you have black pudding?"

Then: "Sorry that sounds mental"

Then: "But do you???"

I'm sat there in my bed, in my dad's old Leicester City shirt that I've had since Year 7 and is definitely getting a bit noncy now but it's comfortable innit, thinking am I having a stroke? Is this what having a stroke feels like? Because what the fuck is happening?

Black pudding? At midnight? After blood emojis?

I text back: "who is this"

Three dots appear immediately. Disappear. Appear. Disappear. This goes on for genuinely five minutes. I'm about to block the number when:

"Felix"

Felix? FELIX? The only Felix I know is that posh new kid who joined Year 12 after Christmas. The one who showed up wearing an actual cravat on his first day like we're in a BBC drama about Eton or something. Madison says he's fit but Madison also fancies Mr. Henderson who's literally forty and has a combover, so her judgment's questionable.

"felix who" I type, even though there's literally only one Felix in our entire shit comprehensive.

"Montague-Ashworth"

Of course he has a double-barreled surname. Of course he does.

"why have u got my number"

Three dots for ages then: "School directory?"

We don't have a school directory. We have a WhatsApp group for PE that everyone's muted and a Facebook page run by someone's mum from 2015. 

"that doesnt exist mate"

"Oh"

"so???"

"I may have asked someone"

"who"

"Rather not say"

Rather not say? RATHER NOT SAY? Who talks like that? It's midnight and this posh twat is texting me about black pudding and using words like 'rather.'

"ur being proper weird"

"I'm aware. Sorry. It's an emergency"

"black pudding emergency?"

"Yes"

"at midnight"

"Yes"

"u having a breakdown?"

Long pause. Then: "Possibly"

Not gonna lie, that made me laugh. At least he's self-aware.

"cant help u mate dads shop shut"

This is a lie. The shop's definitely shut but we've got loads of black pudding in the big freezer because my dad ordered too much for some Polish customer who never came back. But I'm not telling Felix Montague-Hyphen-Whatever that I have access to emergency midnight black pudding because that's how you end up being the Black Pudding Girl for the rest of school.

"Tomorrow?" he texts. "Before school?"

"why"

"Please"

"but WHY"

"I'll explain tomorrow. By the bins. 7:30?"

By the bins? Everything about this screams "you're about to get murdered or recruited into something weird." But also, what else am I doing at 7:30? Pretending to catch the early bus while actually going to Tesco to nick Monster energy?

"ill think about it"

"I'll pay you"

Now he's talking my language. Rich boy wants to pay for black pudding? Fine.

"how much"

"Twenty?"

TWENTY POUNDS? For black pudding that costs like £2.50?

"ur actually mental"

"Yes"

"see u at 7:30"

"Thank you. Really. Thank you so much"

"yeah whatever"

Then, because I can't help myself: "those blood emojis tho?"

Three dots for ages. Then: "Misunderstanding"

"what kind of misunderstanding involves blood emojis"

"The complicated kind"

"that tells me literally nothing"

"I'm aware. Tomorrow. Please bring the black pudding"

I'm about to reply when he adds: "Please don't tell anyone"

"wasnt gonna"

"Thank you"

"stop saying thank you its weird"

"Sor- Okay"

I block his number because I don't need this energy in my life at midnight. Then I unblock it because twenty quid is twenty quid. Then I go back to TikTok but I can't focus because what the actual fuck?

Black pudding emergency. Blood emojis. Felix Montague-Posh-Face.

This is either going to be the weirdest Monday of my life or I'm about to get murdered by a posh vampire. Either way, better than Biology revision.

I text Keya again: "you'll never guess what just happened"

Still no reply. Still at Jordan's. Still gross.

Whatever. I'm going to bed. But first I'm googling "black pudding emergency" just to check it's not some weird sex thing.

It's not.

I think.
step_outputs.7:
# Chapter 2: Felix Is Definitely Something

So I'm stood by the bins at 7:28 Monday morning with three frozen black puddings in my Tesco bag, thinking this is how people get murdered. Like, this is literally the start of a true crime podcast. "She was a promising student who made one fatal mistake - she brought meat products to a strange posh boy at dawn."

The bins proper reek as well because Year 7s had Food Tech on Friday and you know they can't cook for shit. Someone's definitely made tuna pasta bake and it's been festering all weekend.

Then Felix rocks up looking like actual death.

I'm not being dramatic. He looks like he's been sleeping rough, except his uniform's still weirdly perfect? Like creased but expensive creased. His face though - mate, I've seen Year 11s after Reading Festival and they looked better. Proper pale, not white-boy-in-winter pale but concerning pale. Like call-the-school-nurse pale. And his eyes are all red like he's been crying or doing drugs, but let's be real, this boy doesn't know where to buy drugs. He probably thinks you get them from an actual pharmacy.

"You came," he says, and his voice is all scratchy.

"Yeah well twenty quid innit."

He starts fumbling with his wallet - actual leather wallet, not a Velcro Adidas one from Sports Direct - and his hands are shaking so bad he drops it. When he bends to pick it up, I swear he nearly falls over.

"You alright?" I ask, because he's clearly not.

"Fine. Just. The black pudding?"

I hand him the Tesco bag and no word of a lie, his whole face changes. Like when Keya sees there's chocolate cake at lunch. He opens one package right there, doesn't even care it's frozen solid, and starts eating it. Raw. Frozen. By the bins.

"What the fuck," I say.

He's crying. Actually crying. Eating frozen black pudding and crying by the bins at half seven in the morning.

"Sorry," he goes, mouth full. "Sorry, I just. I haven't. It's been three days."

"Since you ate?"

"Since I ate properly."

Properly? What's properly about eating frozen black pudding like it's a Magnum?

"You having a breakdown?" I ask, because someone needs to.

He laughs but it's not a happy laugh. It's the kind of laugh my brother did when he failed his driving test for the third time. "My cousin says I'm having an episode."

"What kind of episode?"

"The embarrassing kind."

He finishes the first black pudding, starts on the second. I'm watching him demolish it thinking this is definitely going in the group chat later except who'd believe me? 'Yeah so the posh new boy paid me twenty quid to eat raw meat by the bins.' Sure Nish.

"So," I say, because the silence is getting weird, "you gonna explain the blood emojis or?"

He stops mid-chew. "That was. Miscommunication."

"With who?"

"Myself."

"You miscommunicated with yourself?"

"I meant to text my cousin. Eugenie. She's. She helps with my dietary restrictions."

"Your dietary restrictions of eating frozen black pudding by bins?"

"It's complicated."

Everything's complicated with this boy. His name's complicated. His hair's complicated - how is it that perfect when he clearly hasn't slept? His whole vibe is complicated.

"Right," I say. "So your cousin Eugenie helps you with your eating disorder-"

"It's not an eating disorder."

"Mate, you're crying over black pudding."

"It's not-" He stops. Takes this proper deep breath like my mum does when I've done something stupid but she's trying not to shout. "I have a condition."

"What condition?"

"The kind that makes me need iron. Lots of iron. Black pudding has iron."

"So does spinach."

He makes this face like I've suggested he eat actual shit. "Spinach doesn't work."

"Why?"

"Because it doesn't."

We're going in circles and the bell's about to go. I can see Madison and her lot coming through the gate, all Love Island hair and vape clouds. If they see me with Felix by the bins, that's it. That's my social life done. Not that I have one, but still.

"Alright, whatever," I say. "You've got your weird meat, I've got my twenty quid, we done?"

He hands me thirty.

"It's twenty."

"Ten extra for not telling anyone."

"I literally don't know what I'd tell anyone. 'Felix eats black pudding'? Riveting."

"Please," he says, and he looks properly desperate. "I know I'm being strange. I know this is. Unconventional. But I'm adjusting."

"To what?"

"The Midlands."

I nearly choke. "The MIDLANDS? That's what's wrong with you? The Midlands?"

"Among other things."

"Where were you before?"

"Surrey."

"And Surrey doesn't have black pudding?"

"Surrey has." He pauses. "Different resources."

Different resources. Who talks like that? 

Madison and her lot are getting closer. I can hear her fake laugh from here.

"I have to go," I say.

"Wait." He grabs my arm and his hand is freezing. Properly ice cold. In February fair enough, but this is through his whole blazer sleeve cold. "Will you. Can I. Tomorrow?"

"Tomorrow what?"

"Bring more?"

"Black pudding?"

"Please."

"You're actually cracked."

"Yes. But I'll pay."

The thing is, thirty quid for frozen meat is the easiest money I've ever made. And it's not like I'm doing anything else before school except avoiding my dad's questions about why I'm up early.

"Same time?"

"You're a lifesaver," he says, and he means it. Like, properly means it. Like I've actually saved his life with dodgy frozen offal from my dad's shop.

"Yeah whatever. Don't make it weird."

But as I'm walking away, I hear him say something mental to himself: "She smells like survival."

What the fuck does that mean?

I look back and he's still stood there, clutching the last black pudding like it's a winning lottery ticket, looking like death but smiling.

Felix Montague-Whatever is definitely something. I just don't know what.
step_outputs.8:
# Chapter 3: This Is Getting Weird Now

Felix won't leave me alone now. It's been a week of black pudding deliveries and he follows me round school like those Year 7s who attach themselves to you because you were nice to them once. Except I wasn't even nice. I sold him processed pig blood for profit.

He doesn't know how anything works. I'm not taking the piss - he genuinely doesn't understand basic school shit. Wednesday he brought a whole roast chicken to lunch in a Waitrose bag. A WHOLE CHICKEN. Tried to pay the dinner ladies with a fifty pound note. They had to get Mr. Henderson to explain the card system while everyone's recording it thinking he's doing some weird TikTok.

"Why's everything so complicated?" he asked me after, properly upset about his chicken situation.

"It's not complicated, you just tap your card and get food."

"But I had food."

"You can't bring outside chicken into the canteen."

"Why?"

"Because you can't."

"But-"

"Felix, why've you got a whole chicken?"

"Protein."

He ended up eating it on the field like some feral Year 9. Madison thought it was "quirky." I thought it was mental but also kind of impressive? Like, eating an entire chicken with your hands while Year 8s play football around you is a power move.

The thing is, he's not eating anything else. Ever. I watch him sometimes (not in a weird way, just in a confused way) and he never eats normal food. Everyone else is having their meal deal from Tesco, crisps in Maths, sharing Tangfastics in English, and he's just sat there with his black pudding. Doesn't even hide it. Just raw-dogs black pudding in Chemistry while we're doing titrations.

"That's minging," Keya said to him yesterday.

"It's medicinal," he replied, dead serious.

Keya laughed because she thinks everything's funny when she's trying not to think about being pregnant (yeah, she told me Tuesday, Jordan doesn't know yet). "Medicinal black pudding? What's it cure, being alive?"

"Something like that," Felix said, and the way he said it made everyone go quiet.

Anyway, so Friday lunch we're sat on the benches by Science and Keya's going on about how her sister swears she saw a big cat in Sainsbury's car park.

"It was like a panther or something," Keya says, showing us her sister's blurry Instagram story that could literally be anything. A bin bag. A Year 11 having a breakdown. Anything.

"There's no panthers in the Midlands," I say.

"Could be escaped from a zoo," Madison suggests, because she thinks she's in a Netflix documentary.

"What zoo?" I ask. "Twycross? They've got meerkats and a sad gorilla, not panthers."

Then Felix, who's been quiet this whole time just systematically destroying his fourth black pudding of the day, goes: "That's just Eugenie hunting."

Everyone stops.

"What?" Keya says.

He looks up like he's just realized he's said it out loud. "Nothing. Family joke."

"Your family jokes about hunting in Sainsbury's car park?" I ask.

"She shops there sometimes."

"Your cousin shops in our Sainsbury's?" Madison perks up because she thinks anyone related to Felix must be proper posh and therefore interesting.

"When she's visiting."

"She visiting now?" I ask, and something about the way his face goes even paler (how?) makes me think this is important.

"Potentially."

"Potentially?"

"She doesn't really announce herself. She just. Appears."

"Like the Sainsbury's panther," Keya laughs.

"Exactly like that," Felix says, but he's not laughing.

The bell goes and everyone disperses to Period 5 but I grab Felix's sleeve. His arm's still freezing, even though it's actually warm today and everyone's complaining about the heating being on.

"What's Eugenie hunting?" I ask.

"Nothing."

"Felix."

"Bargains. She's hunting bargains. There's a reduced section."

"You're lying."

"I'm not."

"You are. You're doing that thing with your face."

"What thing?"

"That thing where you look like you're about to cry or throw up."

He does the face even more. "I need to go to History."

"You don't take History."

"I do now."

"Since when?"

"Since I need to go to History."

He actually legs it. Properly runs away from me, his stupid long legs taking him halfway across the quad before I can even process what's happened. 

But here's the weird bit - and I'm only noticing this now I'm thinking about it - when he ran, Mr. Davies was coming the other way with a mug of tea (he always has tea, it's his whole personality) and Felix moved around him. Not past him. Around him. Like, impossibly around him. Like when you replay a video and realize the physics don't make sense.

After school I wait for him by the gates but he doesn't come out the normal way. I see him briefly, climbing over the back fence by the PE block, the one with actual barbed wire that Year 10s dare each other to climb.

He doesn't even snag his blazer.

I text him: "ur being weird"

He replies instantly: "I'm aware. Bring extra tomorrow."

"extra what"

"Black pudding."

"why"

"Eugenie might need some."

"thought she hunts her own food"

Long pause. Then: "That was a joke."

"didn't feel like a joke"

"Everything feels like something when you're not sleeping properly."

"why aren't u sleeping"

"The Midlands."

"that makes no sense"

"Nothing makes sense. See you tomorrow. 7:30. Please."

I don't reply but we both know I'll be there. Thirty quid is thirty quid, and also I want to know what the fuck is happening with this boy.

Keya texts me later: "felix is deffo weird but like hot weird??? question mark???"

I send back: "he eats raw meat and runs away from conversations"

"so?????? josh harlow eats his own bogies and madison still got with him"

Can't argue with that logic.

But I screenshot Felix's texts and save them in a folder called "Evidence" because if he murders me, at least the police will have something to work with.
step_outputs.9:
# Chapter 4: I Might Be In A Cult?

Skip to Friday because nothing happened except Lewis actually did beat up his stepdad but that's not relevant and also everyone saw it coming. The stepdad pressed charges though which is peak because Lewis is definitely getting expelled now and he was our only chance at winning literally any football match this year.

Anyway, Felix corners me after Biology looking properly nervous. More nervous than when Mrs. Patel asked him to demonstrate osmosis and he just stood there for forty seconds holding a potato.

"Do you want to come to mine after school?"

I look at him. He's got black pudding grease on his collar and his tie's inside out.

"Why?"

"To hang out?"

"We don't hang out."

"We could start."

"Could we though?"

He does this thing where he fidgets with his blazer cuffs. His nails are weirdly perfect, like he gets manicures. Maybe he does. Posh people do mental stuff like that.

"I'll pay for the Uber," he says.

"Uber where?"

"My house."

"Where's your house?"

"Near the bypass."

"Nothing's near the bypass except the Harvester and that creepy old building that's been for sale since Year 4."

"Yes."

"Yes what?"

"The second one."

I stare at him. "You live in the murder house?"

"It's not a murder house."

"Someone definitely died there."

"Multiple people died there. It was built in 1823. Statistically-"

"You live in the murder house by the bypass?"

"It's not ideal."

Not ideal? The place hasn't had windows for a decade. Year 9s go there to do ket and summon demons. My brother lost his virginity there and got chlamydia AND scabies.

"Felix what the fuck."

"Will you come?"

The thing is, I'm obviously going to go because this is insane and I need to see it. Also Keya's getting her nails done with Jordan's mum (weird relationship development but whatever) so I've got nothing else to do.

The Uber driver nearly refuses to take us when Felix gives the address.

"That's not a house, bruv."

"It is," Felix insists.

"It's condemned."

"It's character property."

The driver looks at me like 'is your boyfriend okay?' and I want to die but also thirty quid is thirty quid so I just shrug.

The murder house is worse than I remembered. It's not even trying to be a house anymore. It's just... giving up. There's vegetation growing inside, actual trees coming through the windows. The front door's been replaced with a sheet of plywood that Felix moves aside like it weighs nothing.

"You live here?" I ask.

"Temporarily."

"Felix, there's no roof."

"There's some roof."

"SOME roof?"

Inside is mental. Proper mental. There's camping equipment in what might have been a living room - expensive stuff, like those posh tents from Glastonbury that cost more than my dad's car. But also there's paintings everywhere. Massive oil paintings in gold frames, just propped against the moldy walls. 

"Family portraits," Felix says when he sees me looking.

I get closer to one. It's some Victorian woman in a massive dress looking miserable in front of a horse. But the face...

"That looks exactly like you."

"Family resemblance is strong."

"No, Felix, that looks EXACTLY like you."

"Don't be ridiculous."

But I'm already taking photos because this is definitely going on the group chat. Except when I check my phone there's no signal. Of course there's no signal in the murder house.

"How do you charge your phone?" I ask.

"There's a generator."

"Where?"

He waves vaguely at nothing.

I'm looking at more paintings now and they're all him. Different clothes, different time periods, but it's his face. His exact face. There's one of a soldier from like World War One that has his same weird perfect nose.

"These are all you."

"They're relatives."

"Felix."

"Strong genes."

"FELIX."

He's not looking at me, instead unpacking a Tesco bag full of black pudding onto a camping table. His weekend supply, I guess. The table's got other stuff on it - papers in languages I don't recognize, what looks like a proper old map of the Midlands where our town doesn't even exist yet, and a jewelry box full of teeth.

"Why've you got teeth?"

"They're not mine."

"That's not better."

"They're antique."

"ANTIQUE TEETH?"

He's getting stressed now, doing that thing where he looks like he might cry. "This was a mistake. I shouldn't have brought you here."

"You think?"

"I just wanted to hang out normally."

"This isn't normal! Nothing about this is normal! You live in a condemned building with paintings of yourself from the Victorian era and a box of teeth!"

"They're not all Victorian. Some are Edwardian."

I can't. I actually can't.

"I'm leaving."

"Wait." He grabs my arm with his freezing hands. "Please. I know it's weird. I know I'm weird. But I just... I don't have anyone else to talk to."

And the thing is, he looks so pathetic standing there in this rotting house with his black pudding and his paintings and his antique teeth that I actually feel bad for him.

"Are you in a cult?" I ask.

"What?"

"Is this a cult thing? The paintings and the teeth and the murder house?"

"It's not a murder house."

"That's not a no to the cult question."

He thinks about it. Actually properly considers it. "I don't think so."

"You don't THINK so?"

"Eugenie says it's more of a family tradition."

"What tradition?"

"Existing."

Before I can ask what the fuck that means, there's a noise from upstairs. Footsteps. In a house with no proper stairs.

"That'll be her," Felix says, and he looks terrified.

"Who?"

"Eugenie. She said she might drop by."

"Drop by? Through the ceiling?"

The footsteps get closer. On the ceiling now. Actually on the ceiling.

"We should go," Felix says.

"You THINK?"

We proper leg it out of there. I don't look back but I swear I see something moving in the upstairs window that doesn't exist.

The Uber back costs forty quid and Felix pays without blinking. I get home and immediately reverse image search the paintings.

One's in the Leicester Museum. "Portrait of Unknown Young Woman, 1847." Another's in a book about local history. "The Montague Family, 1892."

All of them have Felix's face.

All of them.

What the fuck have I gotten myself into?
step_outputs.10:
# Chapter 5: Madison's Party Was Carnage

Everyone knows about Madison's party where the police came but nobody tells it right so let me set the record straight.

First off, Madison's mum was already hammered when we arrived. Proper wine-mum wasted, wearing a dress from ASOS that was clearly meant for Madison, trying to add everyone on Instagram. She kept calling Felix "Edward" and touching his hair which is definitely noncey considering we're literally sixteen and she's like forty-something pretending to be thirty.

"You're so pale," she kept saying. "Do you not go outside?"

Felix just stood there frozen like when you see a teacher in Tesco and don't know how to act.

The house was already rammed. Year 11s everywhere, couple of Year 10s who definitely weren't invited, and someone's older brother who left school three years ago but still comes to these things which is tragic. Everyone's drinking whatever they could nick from their parents - so basically Glenn's vodka and wine that tastes like vinegar.

Keya's already fucked and it's only nine o'clock. She's crying in the bathroom about Jordan but also eating a share bag of Doritos so she's fine. I check on her and she goes "I think I'm pregnant" and I'm like "yeah you told me Tuesday" and she's like "did I?" This is why you shouldn't drink when you're pregnant but I'm not her mum so.

Felix hasn't drunk anything because he "doesn't drink" which is the most suspicious thing he's said yet. What seventeen-year-old doesn't drink? Even the Muslim kids drink, they just don't tell their parents.

"You're making everyone nervous," I tell him.

"Why?"

"Because you're sober and staring at people."

"I'm not staring."

"You're literally staring at Tom's neck right now."

"He has an interesting jugular."

"That's the weirdest thing you've ever said and you once asked me if blood comes in different flavors."

He stops staring at Tom's neck and stares at mine instead which is worse.

Madison appears with her usual drama, announcing that someone's definitely pregnant because she found a test in the bins. Everyone looks at Yasmin who goes bright red but I know it's Keya's because she did three tests in Madison's bathroom and didn't even wrap them in tissue, just lobbed them straight in the bin because she has no sense of self-preservation.

"It's not mine," Yasmin says, and everyone believes her because she's never even held hands with anyone.

"Whose is it then?" Madison's loving this. She lives for drama. Last year she started a rumor about herself just to see what would happen.

Nobody admits anything so Madison gets bored and puts on music so loud the neighbors definitely hate us.

By ten, it's already messy. Someone's smashed a vase and hidden the pieces under the sofa. Lewis's cousin is selling ket in the garden even though Lewis isn't here because of the stepdad situation. A Year 10 is crying because another Year 10got with her ex. Standard party stuff.

Felix and I end up in the garden because he says he needs air even though we're literally outside. Jordan Fletcher's already tactical chundering in the koi pond which is disgusting but also impressive forward planning.

"I can't do this," Felix says.

"Can't do what?"

"This." He gestures at everything. "All these people. The smells. The sounds. The necks."

"Stop saying necks."

"Sorry."

"Do you want to leave?"

"I can't. I promised Madison I'd stay til eleven."

"Why?"

"She said I was mysterious and leaving early would be 'on brand.'"

I'm about to tell him that Madison's full of shit when someone screams. Proper horror movie screams.

Everyone rushes to look and there's a whole-ass woman in the tree. Not a girl, a proper adult woman, just sitting in Madison's mum's oak tree wearing what looks like a ballgown from the Victorian era but also might be from Zara's new collection.

She's just sitting there, watching the party.

"What the fuck?" someone says.

"Is that your mum?" someone asks Tom.

"Why would my mum be in a tree?"

"Performance art?" suggests Madison, because she did drama GCSE and thinks everything's performance art.

Felix goes white. Whiter. Translucent.

"That's Eugenie," he says, but only I hear him.

The woman in the tree - Eugenie - waves. Not a normal wave. A little finger wiggle like she's at the opera or something.

"I'm calling the police," Madison's mum slurs from the patio door.

"Don't!" Felix actually shouts, which makes everyone look at him. "She's... she's my cousin."

"Your cousin's in a tree?" Madison asks.

"She's eccentric."

"Eccentric?"

"She went to Oxford."

Everyone nods like this explains everything because posh people are mental and we all know it.

But Eugenie's already gone. Not climbed down gone. Just gone. The tree's empty and everyone's arguing about whether we imagined it and Jordan's going "there was a fit woman in the tree" even though he's still being sick.

The police arrive about five minutes later because of the noise complaint, not the tree woman. Madison's mum tries to flirt with them which goes about as well as you'd expect. They make everyone go home and take Jordan's brother's details because he's twenty and shouldn't be at a Year 11 party.

Felix proper legs it when he sees the police cars. Actually runs away, leaving me to walk home alone which is shit friend behavior even if we're not actually friends.

I get about halfway home when I see her. Eugenie. Standing outside Tesco Express like she's waiting for an Uber, except she's definitely not because she's holding what looks like a rabbit but might be a cat. It's hard to tell because it's not moving.

She sees me and smiles. She's got Felix's face but older, sharper, like someone took his features and made them mean.

"You're the one helping Felix with his situation," she says. Not a question.

"What situation?"

"His dietary requirements."

Why does everyone keep saying that?

"I sell him black pudding."

"Yes." She says it like it means something else. "Make sure he feeds properly."

"On black pudding?"

"On whatever keeps him stable."

She hands me twenty quid. Just straight up gives me money like I'm a dealer or something.

"I don't-"

"For your discretion."

Then she's gone. And I mean gone. I look down at the twenty quid for literally a second and when I look up she's vanished. The Tesco security cameras probably caught it but who's gonna check?

I text Felix: "ur cousin is mental"

He texts back immediately: "I'm so sorry"

"she gave me £20"

"Keep it"

"why"

"Compensation"

"for what"

He doesn't reply.

Sunday I text him twelve times but he blanks me. Keya texts saying Jordan found out about the baby and he's actually happy which is mental but also kind of sweet I guess.

Monday morning Felix isn't at the bins. His phone's off.

Tuesday I hear from Madison that he's off sick.

Wednesday his name's not on the register anymore.

What the fuck.
step_outputs.11:
# Chapter 6: Shit Gets Weirder

Felix texts me sixty times Sunday. I'm not exaggerating. Sixty. I counted. All variations of "I'm sorry" and "please let me explain" and "Eugenie isn't usually like that" and "are you okay" and one that just says "trees are not her normal approach."

I ignore all of them because I've got Biology revision and my dad's doing my head in about working in the shop Monday morning before school. "You never help anymore," he goes, like I don't work every Sunday and two evenings a week for literally no money except what I nick from the till.

"I've got GCSEs," I tell him.

"Your brother managed GCSEs and working."

"Yeah and he got all 4s didn't he?"

My mum clips me round the head for that but gentle-like because she knows I'm right. Dip's thick as shit and working at Halfords now so not exactly winning at life.

Monday morning I'm trying to restock the crisps (we've started doing those posh Tyrrell's ones because gentrification's coming even to our shit area) when Felix appears at the shop door looking like he's been sleeping in a bin.

"We're not open," I say through the glass.

"Please."

"Opens at seven."

"Nish, please."

My dad appears behind me. "Is this your boyfriend?"

"No."

"He looks unwell."

"He always looks like that."

"Does he want black pudding?"

I'm going to kill my dad. Actually kill him. Felix's face does something complicated.

"We're out," I lie.

"No we're not," Dad says, because he's a traitor. "We've got loads. Too much. You want some, son?"

Felix nods like a drowning man being offered a life jacket.

Dad lets him in because my dad has no survival instincts and would definitely die first in a horror film. Felix buys eight packages of black pudding and demolishes one right there at the counter like it's a Kit Kat.

"You weren't at school," I say.

"I was dealing with things."

"What things?"

"Family things."

Dad's watching this like it's EastEnders. "You two have a falling out?"

"No," I say.

"Yes," Felix says.

"Which is it?"

"His cousin's mental," I tell Dad.

"Whose isn't?" Dad says, which fair point. Our cousin Raj thinks he's going to be on Love Island even though he looks like a potato with anxiety.

Felix follows me to school which I can't stop him doing because it's a free country innit. He properly corners me behind the languages block where Year 9s go to vape and there's that weird smell that might be cat piss or might be Leon's lynx Africa.

"Did Eugenie talk to you?" he asks, urgent.

"Yeah, she's mental. Runs in the family clearly."

"What did she say exactly?"

"Told me to make sure you eat."

His whole body relaxes. "That's all?"

"Yeah. No wait, she said make sure you feed properly."

"Did she say what specifically?"

"No, but I assumed black pudding because you're obsessed."

He looks so relieved I think he might cry. "She didn't mention the original arrangement?"

"What arrangement?"

"Nothing. It doesn't matter."

"Felix, what arrangement?"

But then the fire alarm goes off because of course it does. It's always the fire alarm. If there was an actual fire we'd all die because nobody takes it seriously anymore. We trudge out to the tennis courts for registration and Mr. Henderson's doing a head count but everyone knows it's just Year 10s vaping in the disabled toilets again. They never learn.

"What arrangement?" I ask again but Felix pretends he can't hear me over everyone complaining about the cold.

He avoids me the rest of the day. Literally turns around when he sees me coming. Sits on the other side of the classroom. At lunch he's nowhere, probably eating black pudding in a cupboard somewhere like the freak he is.

Keya finds me after school. "You and Felix had a domestic?"

"We're not together."

"Madison says you are."

"Madison thinks she saw Harry Styles in Primark last week."

"Maybe she did."

"In our Primark? In the Midlands? Harry Styles?"

"Could happen."

This is why Keya's pregnant. No critical thinking skills.

That night I'm in bed scrolling TikTok when I get a text. Three blood drops. 🩸🩸🩸

Same as before but it's a different number.

Then: "He's getting hungry."

I text back: "who is this"

"You know who this is."

"eugenie?"

"Feed him."

"he feeds himself like a big boy"

"Not properly."

"not my problem"

"It will be."

Then she sends a photo. It's me at the shop this morning, taken from outside. You can see Felix at the counter and me looking pissed off and my dad in his embarrassing Leicester City pajama bottoms he wears under his trousers when it's cold.

"are you stalking me???"

"Feed him properly or I will."

"that a threat?"

"It's a promise."

I block the number immediately. Fuck this. Fuck Felix and his mental cousin and their weird black pudding cult or whatever this is.

But I screenshot everything first because I'm not stupid.

Tuesday Felix is back at the bins before school.

"I need more," he says.

"Your cousin's threatening me."

"What?"

I show him the texts. His face does that thing where he looks like he might throw up.

"Oh no."

"Oh no what?"

"She's escalating."

"Escalating what?"

"The situation."

"WHAT SITUATION?"

"I can't tell you."

"Why?"

"Because you'll run."

"I'm already running. This is me running. Your cousin's in trees and taking creepy photos and sending me threatening texts about feeding you."

"I know."

"So tell me what's happening."

"I can't."

"Felix."

"I literally can't. She'll know. She always knows."

The thing is, he looks properly scared. Not worried or anxious, but scared like when my brother saw our dad cry that one time.

"Is she dangerous?" I ask.

"Yes."

"To me?"

"To everyone."

"Felix, what the fuck?"

"Just keep bringing me black pudding. Please. I'll pay double. Triple. Whatever. Just until I figure this out."

"Figure what out?"

But he's already walking away, clutching his Tesco bag of black pudding like it's keeping him alive.

Wednesday I get another text: "He's getting hungrier."

Thursday: "Soon he won't be able to control it."

Friday morning: "Last chance."

I don't tell Felix about these texts. I don't know why. Maybe because he already looks like he's dying and I don't want to be responsible for pushing him over the edge.

Or maybe because I'm starting to think Eugenie's right.

Whatever's happening with Felix, it's getting worse.

And I'm right in the middle of it.
step_outputs.12:
# Chapter 7: Shit Gets Weirder

So that was three weeks ago and honestly I don't know what happened.

Felix disappeared. Like properly disappeared, not just off school for a sick day disappeared. His name's not on any register, his phone's dead, even his shit's gone from the murder house (I checked, don't judge me).

Madison's parents are getting divorced because of the party but really because her mum's been shagging the builder since January. The party was just the excuse her dad needed. Madison's pretending she's traumatized but she's already planning her two birthdays two Christmases angle.

Keya's pregnant. Not Yasmin, which everyone assumed because Yasmin gained weight. Turns out Yasmin just discovered Uber Eats. Keya told everyone at lunch Tuesday, casual as anything, eating chips like "yeah so I'm keeping it." Jordan actually cried. Not cute crying either, proper ugly crying into his Subway sandwich. They're looking at flats now which is mental because neither of them can even drive.

But that's all normal shit compared to what's happening to me.

I'm still getting the texts. Different number every time so blocking's pointless. Started simple - just blood drops. Then words:

"He's getting hungrier"
"Feed him"
"You promised"
"He needs you"
"Time's running out"

I never promised shit. I sold black pudding to a weird posh boy for profit. That's not a blood oath or whatever this is.

The paintings disappeared. I mean they're gone from the internet. I had screenshots on my phone - that Victorian woman with Felix's face, the soldier, all of them. Went to show Keya and they're corrupted files. The museum website's got nothing. Even that local history book I found on Google Books - the pages are missing. Just gone. "File not found." How does that even happen?

Thursday the murder house burned down. Nobody reported it. I only know because I was on the bus to Tesco and saw the smoke. By the time I got there (yes I got off the bus to look, shut up) it was just black bones of building and no fire brigade, no police, nothing. Like it burned itself quietly without telling anyone.

There's FOR SALE signs up already. "Development opportunity." My brother says they're building flats but my brother also thinks he's going to win the lottery so.

I see Eugenie sometimes. Always in Tesco Express, always at the reduced section. Last week she bought twelve packages of black pudding, all the ones with yellow stickers. Winked at me as she left. The security guard didn't even look at her. Nobody ever looks at her except me.

"That your mate's cousin?" the guy on the till asked.

"How'd you know?"

"Looks just like him innit. But fit."

Which is gross because she's old (like thirty) but also true. She does look exactly like Felix if Felix wasn't malnourished and actually knew how to do his hair.

My dad thinks I'm on drugs now because black pudding keeps going missing and I can't explain it. I'm not even taking it anymore - it's just disappearing. We had twenty packages Monday morning, by Wednesday there's three.

"Are you selling them?" Dad asks.

"To who? The black pudding dealers?"

"Don't be smart."

"Don't be stupid."

Mum clips me for that but she's worried too. Started checking my arms for track marks like I'm in a Channel 4 documentary about youth gone wrong.

The thing that nobody knows - that I'm not telling anyone - is I got my GCSE results early because of some admin fuck up. Straight 9s. Even in French and I don't speak French. The email said "exceptional circumstances" but wouldn't say what circumstances.

Birmingham offered me a place for Medicine starting September. Full ride. Scholarship and everything. They never give scholarships to people like me. People from here. But there it is in writing - "exceptional candidate" and "unique potential" and "delighted to offer."

I should be buzzing. I should be telling everyone. This is my ticket out, everything I wanted.

But I'm not going.

Because last night I found something under my pillow. A tooth. Old, like propery old. Wrapped in paper that had one word: "Soon."

I know it's from Felix. Don't ask me how, I just know. The tooth is exactly like the ones in his jewelry box, and it smells like him - that weird rust and expensive cologne smell.

He's coming back. Or he never left. Or something.

Keya thinks I'm having a breakdown. "You've been proper weird since that posh boy left."

"I'm fine."

"You're not eating."

"I'm eating."

"You're not. And you keep looking at people's necks."

Fuck. I am. I'm doing what Felix did, staring at jugulars like they're interesting. Tom's got a particularly good one. Very visible. Very accessible.

"It's stress," I tell her.

"What stress? You already got into Birmingham."

"How do you know that?"

"Your mum told my mum told everyone."

Of course she did.

The texts are getting more frequent now. Sometimes just coordinates. I looked them up once - it's the bypass, right where the murder house was. Sometimes it's times and dates. Tomorrow, midnight. Sunday, 3am. I never go but they keep coming.

Today's was different.

"He's so hungry he's forgotten how to be human."

Then a photo. It's dark and blurry but there's something in it. A shape in a tree. Could be anything. Could be a big cat. Could be Felix.

Could be nothing.

I'm going mad. That's what this is. Stress-induced psychosis from GCSEs and my parents and Keya's pregnancy drama. There's no vampires, no Eugenie, no Felix in trees. Just a weird boy who moved away and my brain filling in gaps with horror film shit.

But I keep the tooth under my pillow.

Just in case.
step_outputs.13:
# Chapter 8: I Still Don't Know

So that was three weeks ago and I honestly don't know what happened.

I'm at the shop right now, it's Thursday afternoon, meant to be revising for Biology retakes even though I got a 9 but Mum doesn't believe me. Dad's in the back arguing with the Walkers delivery guy about cheese and onion proportions. Standard Thursday really.

Except I've got twelve packages of black pudding in my school bag.

Not selling them. Not eating them. Just... having them. Like how some people carry lucky pennies or whatever. Mine just happen to be processed pig blood that cost me thirty quid I don't have.

Keya comes in around four, massive now, waddling like those women on One Born Every Minute. Jordan's with her carrying her bags like a proper boyfriend which nobody saw coming.

"You alright?" she asks.

"Yeah why?"

"You're staring at my neck."

"No I'm not."

"You literally are."

She's right. I am. It's got this vein that's properly visible and I can see her pulse and it's weirdly calming? That's mental. I'm mental.

"Sorry. Tired."

"You're always tired now."

"GCSEs innit."

"They're over."

"Retakes."

"You got all 9s."

"How does everyone know that?"

"Your mum put it on Facebook."

Kill me.

Jordan's buying energy drinks even though Keya's not meant to have caffeine but I don't serve him because I see Eugenie outside. She's just standing there on the pavement, looking at our shop window like there's something interesting about our display of out-of-date Pot Noodles.

"Back in a sec," I tell Keya.

Outside, Eugenie smells expensive. Proper rich person smell, like shops you're scared to go in.

"He's coming back," she says.

"Who?"

"Don't be obtuse."

"Felix?"

"He's very hungry."

"Not my problem."

"You still have feelings for him."

"I never had feelings for him. He was just some weird posh boy who ate black pudding."

She smiles. It's not a nice smile. "You carry black pudding in your bag."

How does she know that?

"Coincidence."

"Twelve packages is not coincidence. It's preparation."

"For what?"

"For when he comes back wrong."

"Wrong how?"

But she's already walking away. Not vanishing this time, just walking like a normal person except nobody looks at her. Jordan comes out and walks straight through where she was standing, doesn't even notice.

That night I can't sleep. Keep checking my phone for texts but nothing comes. The tooth under my pillow feels warm, which is impossible but there it is.

3am I hear something outside. Not a cat. Bigger.

I don't look.

Friday's when it happens. I'm in Biology (retaking even though I don't need to but school admin's fucked) when the new kid walks in.

It's Felix but it's not Felix.

Same face, same stupid posh boy hair, same walk like he's underwater. But his eyes are different. Hungrier. And he's too pale even for February Felix standards.

"Class, this is Francis," Mrs. Patel says. "He's just transferred."

Francis. Not Felix. Francis.

He sits right next to me even though there's loads of empty seats.

"Hi," he says, and his voice is Felix's voice but colder.

"Hi."

"You smell like black pudding."

What the fuck do you say to that?

"I work in a shop."

"No," he says. "You smell like you're carrying it. Twelve packages. In your bag."

I look at him properly. Same face. Exactly the same face. But Felix had this softness, this anxiety, this trying-too-hard-to-be-human thing. Francis doesn't have that. Francis looks like he knows exactly what he is.

"Want some?" I ask, because what else do I do?

"Not yet."

"When?"

"When you offer it properly."

Mrs. Patel's droning about cell division but all I can focus on is not-Felix beside me, smelling like rust and old money and something else. Something wrong.

At lunch he follows me. Not like Felix did, all awkward and apologetic. This is deliberate. Predatory.

"You knew him," he says. Not a question.

"Who?"

"My cousin. Felix."

"Cousin?"

"Distant. Very distant."

"Right."

"He talked about you."

"Did he?"

"Said you saved him."

"I sold him black pudding."

"Same thing, in his case."

We're by the bins now, the same bins where this all started. Francis is standing exactly where Felix stood that first morning, but he's not shaking. He's perfectly still. Too still. Nothing about him moves except his mouth.

"Is he coming back?" I ask.

"Do you want him to?"

"I don't care."

"Liar."

"I'm not—"

"You kept the tooth."

"How do you—"

"I can smell it on you. Old calcium and desperation."

That's properly mental but also accurate? The tooth does smell desperate, if desperation has a smell.

"What are you?" I ask.

"Hungry."

"That's not an answer."

"It's the only answer that matters."

He leans closer and his breath is cold, like opening a freezer. "He was weak. Couldn't do what was necessary. But I'm not weak."

"What's necessary?"

"You'll see."

Then he's gone. Not walked away, not even vampire-vanished. Just gone between one blink and the next.

I've still got the twelve black puddings in my bag. They're getting heavy.

That night I get one text: "Sunday. Midnight. Where it started."

The bins. That's where it started.

I'm not going. Obviously I'm not going. I'm not stupid. You don't meet maybe-vampires at midnight by bins. That's how you end up on true crime podcasts.

But I put the tooth in my pocket. And I charge my phone. And I set an alarm for 11:45.

Just in case.

Because here's the thing nobody tells you about maybe-vampires: even when they're gone, even when you know they're dangerous, even when every logical part of your brain is screaming run—

You still want to know what happens next.

And I've got twelve packages of black pudding and nothing to lose except my life, which in this shit town isn't worth much anyway.

Keya texts: "you coming out tomorrow?"

I text back: "maybe. depends."

"on what?"

Good question. Depends on whether Felix is Felix or Francis or something else. Depends on whether Eugenie's threats are real. Depends on whether I'm brave or stupid or both.

Depends on whether vampires are real or I'm just having a very specific mental breakdown involving black pudding and posh boys with eating disorders.

My dad knocks on my door. "You alright? You've been quiet."

"I'm fine."

"Your mum's worried."

"Mum's always worried."

"This is different worried."

"I'm fine."

"Alright. Don't stay up too late."

It's 11:30 now. Fifteen minutes to decide.

The tooth is warm in my pocket. The black pudding's in my bag. My phone's charged.

I know what I'm going to do. Have known all along, probably. Since that first text, those three blood drops, I've been heading towards this moment.

Whatever Felix is, whatever Francis is, whatever's waiting at the bins—

Actually wait, someone's at the door. At midnight? Dad's going mental, shouting about the time. But I can hear the voice and it's

[Analysis]

This chapter demonstrates a strong command of the first-person teenage voice the plan calls for. Nish's narration is consistently rendered - the self-interruptions, the casual cruelty, the mercenary logic ("thirty quid for frozen meat is the easiest money I've ever made"), the social anxiety about Madison seeing her by the bins. The voice feels genuinely inhabited rather than performed.

The dialogue is one of the chapter's strongest elements. The exchange about spinach, the "miscommunicated with yourself" bit, the "adjusting to the Midlands" reveal - these land with genuine comic timing. Felix's speech patterns ("Different resources," "She smells like survival") do the work of suggesting something off without overplaying it. The dialogue feels like actual teenagers (and one possibly-not-teenager) rather than a writer's approximation of teenagers.

The chapter follows its plan faithfully: Felix appears looking rough, eats frozen black pudding and cries, explains "dietary restrictions," pays Nish extra for silence, and ends with the ambiguous "she smells like survival" line. The plan's beats are all hit without feeling mechanical.

Character consistency is strong. Felix is catastrophically lonely, apologetic, speaks like he swallowed a dictionary, and his desperation over the black pudding reads as both comic and genuinely sad. Nish is mercenary, judgmental (the "doesn't know where to buy drugs, probably thinks you get them from an actual pharmacy" line), socially anxious about Madison, and ultimately motivated by money rather than kindness. This is exactly the character profile.

The "she smells like survival" line is the chapter's best moment - it's genuinely unsettling, lands the vampire ambiguity perfectly, and is earned by the preceding scene rather than dropped in arbitrarily.

Where the chapter is slightly weaker: the bins-smell opening paragraph is functional but not particularly inspired. The "like when Keya sees there's chocolate cake at lunch" simile is fine but a bit flat. The chapter doesn't quite achieve the "genuinely mean British teenage behavior" the plan promises - Nish is more wryly observational than mean here, which is appropriate for a chapter where she's mostly alone with Felix, but the edge is slightly softer than the plan suggests.

The "true crime podcast" framing at the opening is a nice touch that fits Nish's character (she wants to do true crime podcasts per her profile). The detail about the Year 7 tuna pasta bake is exactly the kind of specific, grounding detail the plan calls for.

There's no purple prose, no forced metaphors, no tell-don't-show problems of note. The writing is lean and purposeful. The darkness (Felix's genuine desperation, the concerning paleness, the crying) is earned by the comic setup rather than imposed on it.

Minor quibble: "Like call-the-school-nurse pale" is slightly clunky as a construction, and the repetition of "complicated" in the middle section is intentional but runs slightly long. These are small issues in an otherwise well-executed chapter.

This chapter is a strong execution of a very specific and difficult voice. The model has successfully inhabited Nish's perspective with genuine authenticity - the parenthetical asides, the self-interrupting logic, the casual cruelty toward Keya ("probably getting fingered on his nan's sofa while she's upstairs watching Corrie on catch-up"), and the mercenary pivot when Felix mentions money all feel genuinely teenage and genuinely British without being performed or condescending.

The voice is the chapter's greatest strength. It doesn't feel like an adult writing a teenager; it feels like a teenager who thinks she's funnier than she is, which is exactly what the plan called for. The list format (a/b/c/d) for theorizing about the text is a nice structural choice that reveals character efficiently. The detail about being the "unofficial Period Supplier for Year 9s" is exactly the kind of specific, mundane, slightly mercenary detail that makes the world feel real.

The text exchange is well-handled. Felix's voice comes through even in brief texts - "Rather not say" is perfect, and Nish's incredulous reaction to it ("Who talks like that?") is earned. The dialogue does real work: it establishes Felix as genuinely odd without over-explaining, maintains mystery, and advances the plot while being funny. The rhythm of the texts - dots appearing and disappearing, the cascade of messages - is rendered convincingly.

The chapter follows its plan closely. The midnight text, the period emergency theory, the Lewis speculation, the black pudding request, the twenty quid negotiation, the blocking-then-unblocking - all present and executed well. The ending with the Google search is a good button.

Some minor weaknesses: the "either going to be the weirdest Monday of my life or I'm about to get murdered by a posh vampire" line is slightly too on-the-nose given the story's premise - it's a bit of a wink that undercuts the ambiguity the plan wants to maintain. The chapter is also almost entirely setup, which is appropriate for Chapter 1 but means there's limited emotional depth to assess. The "noncy" comment about the Leicester City shirt is a good character detail but the word choice might read as jarring to some audiences (though it's authentically British teen vernacular).

The darkness is well-earned in small ways - the Keya situation, the casual theft, the mercenary attitude - without being heavy-handed. The lightness is genuine rather than forced. This chapter demonstrates a remarkably confident command of voice and character. The first-person narration through Nish is consistently realized - she's judgmental, self-aware in patches, funny in the way she thinks she's funnier than she is, and genuinely observant in ways that feel earned rather than performed. The chapter successfully executes the plan's core intention: making everyone a bit shit while keeping the vampire ambiguity alive.

The dialogue is a genuine strength. The chicken exchange ("But I had food." / "You can't bring outside chicken into the canteen." / "Why?" / "Because you can't.") captures the specific texture of teenage conversation without feeling scripted. Felix's voice is appropriately off - Victorian politeness colliding with modern school life - and the "medicinal black pudding" exchange lands well. Keya's pregnancy reveal is dropped casually mid-paragraph, exactly as Nish would deliver it, which is a nice structural choice. The texting exchange at the end maintains the voice throughout and the "Evidence" folder gag is genuinely funny rather than trying-to-be-funny.

The chapter follows its plan closely: Felix's social incompetence (the chicken, the card system), the Eugenie/panther moment, the "That's just Eugenie hunting" line, and the general escalation of weirdness. The detail about Felix moving "around" Mr. Davies in a physically impossible way is well-placed - specific enough to be unsettling without being heavy-handed.

Where the chapter is slightly weaker: the "impossibly around him. Like when you replay a video and realize the physics don't make sense" moment is the closest thing to a forced metaphor, and it's a bit clunky. The observation about Felix not eating is told more than shown - we're told he never eats normal food rather than seeing it in action. The barbed wire fence moment is good but could be sharper.

The darkness is well-earned - Keya's pregnancy dropped as an aside, Felix's genuine distress about Eugenie, the "something like that" response to "what's it cure, being alive?" - these land because they're not telegraphed. The lightness (the chicken power move, Josh Harlow eating bogies) works because it's grounded in specific, believable teenage logic.

The chapter is coherent, maintains internal consistency, and advances the plot while deepening the mystery without resolving anything. Characters are consistent with their profiles. The voice never slips into adult-writing-as-teenager territory, which is the most common failure mode for this type of fiction.

Minor weaknesses: a couple of moments where the narration tells us how to feel about something rather than letting it land (the "something about the way his face goes even paler (how?) makes me think this is important" is slightly over-explaining). The chapter could be slightly tighter in the middle section. But these are minor complaints against a chapter that largely succeeds at something genuinely difficult.

VOICE AND CHARACTER: Nish's voice is remarkably consistent and convincing. The parenthetical asides ("This is why you shouldn't drink when you're pregnant but I'm not her mum so"), the casual cruelty ("Even the Muslim kids drink, they just don't tell their parents"), and the deflating self-awareness all ring true. DIALOGUE: The dialogue is one of the chapter's genuine strengths. The exchange about staring at necks is economical and funny. "She went to Oxford" / "Everyone nods like this explains everything" is a sharp social observation. The Felix/Nish texting exchange at the end is perfectly calibrated - his one-word responses building dread without melodrama.

PLOT EXECUTION: The chapter follows its plan closely - Madison's mum being drunk and noncey, Felix not drinking, the koi pond vomiting, Eugenie in the tree, the police, Eugenie outside Tesco, the twenty quid, Felix disappearing. The plan is executed faithfully and with good pacing. The chapter ends on a genuinely unsettling note (Felix vanishing from the register) that earns its darkness without overselling it.

SHOW VS TELL: The chapter mostly shows rather than tells. Madison's character is established through action ("Last year she started a rumor about herself just to see what would happen"). The party chaos is rendered through specific, grounded details rather than summary. However, there are moments where Nish editorializes a bit heavily - "Standard party stuff" is a slight cheat, though it's in-voice.

CREATIVITY: The details are specific and fresh - the pregnancy test not wrapped in tissue, Jordan's "tactical chundering" as "impressive forward planning," the Zara/Victorian ballgown ambiguity. These feel genuinely observed rather than generic. The "Oxford" explanation for tree-sitting is a lovely comic beat.

WEAKNESSES: The chapter is perhaps slightly too competent - it executes the plan so faithfully that it occasionally feels like a checklist being ticked. The Keya pregnancy subplot is handled a bit perfunctorily. The ending (Felix disappearing from the register) is effective but the transition from party to aftermath is slightly rushed. The "Eugenie's already gone. Not climbed down gone. Just gone" construction is used twice in close proximity (once for the tree, once for outside Tesco), which is a minor repetition issue.

PURPLE PROSE/FORCED METAPHOR: Essentially none. The prose is deliberately flat and colloquial, which is appropriate and well-executed. No unearned poeticism.

AMATEURISH ELEMENTS: Very few. The writing is confident and controlled. The unreliable narrator device is handled with genuine sophistication - Nish's contradictions and digressions feel organic rather than mechanical.

OVERALL: This is a genuinely accomplished piece of YA darkly comic fiction. It's funny, unsettling, and character-consistent throughout. The vampire ambiguity is maintained perfectly. The chapter earns its moments of genuine weirdness by grounding them in specific, believable social detail.

[Analysis]

Chapter 8 is the concluding chapter of this darkly comic British YA vampire story, and it largely succeeds at what it sets out to do: maintain the voice, deepen the mystery without resolving it, and end mid-sentence in a way that feels earned rather than lazy.

VOICE AND CHARACTER: Nish's voice is remarkably consistent throughout. The self-interruptions, the casual cruelty, the deflection through humor—"Kill me" after her mum posts her grades on Facebook—all feel authentic. The detail about staring at Keya's neck and finding it "weirdly calming" is a genuinely unsettling character beat that shows Nish being changed by her proximity to whatever Felix/Francis is, without the story ever confirming anything supernatural. The line "That's mental. I'm mental." is perfectly calibrated to her voice.

The introduction of Francis as a darker, more predatory version of Felix is clever—it raises the stakes while maintaining ambiguity. Is Francis a different vampire? Felix having "come back wrong"? A delusion? The story wisely refuses to answer. Francis's dialogue is appropriately chilling: "Not yet" and "When you offer it properly" have genuine menace without tipping into camp.

PLOT AND STRUCTURE: The chapter follows the plan closely—three weeks have passed, Felix has disappeared, Nish is still carrying black pudding, Eugenie appears again, and a new/different Felix shows up. The plan mentioned "Francis" as a development and the chapter delivers this effectively. The midnight cliffhanger ending mid-sentence is exactly what was planned and works well as a structural choice.

DIALOGUE: The dialogue is one of the chapter's strongest elements. The Keya exchange about staring at her neck is economical and funny. Francis's lines are genuinely unsettling. The dad knocking on the door is touching in its mundanity. The weakness is that some of Francis's lines veer slightly toward generic vampire menace ("I'm not weak," "You'll see") but this is minor.

SHOW VS. TELL: The chapter mostly shows rather than tells. The twelve black puddings as a character detail is excellent showing. However, the final paragraphs before the cliffhanger become more tell-heavy: "I know what I'm going to do. Have known all along, probably." This is the chapter's weakest section—it over-explains Nish's emotional state in a way that undercuts the voice.

CREATIVITY: The "tooth under my pillow" detail is intriguing and unexplained—where did it come from? This is either a brilliant unexplained detail or a continuity error from the plan (the plan doesn't mention a tooth). It reads as intentionally mysterious but could confuse readers who haven't seen the full story. The "Francis not Felix" twist is genuinely creative and unsettling.

WEAKNESSES: The final pre-cliffhanger paragraphs are the chapter's low point. "Whatever Felix is, whatever Francis is, whatever's waiting at the bins—" followed by "Actually wait, someone's at the door" is a bit too cute. The philosophical musing about wanting to know what happens next ("You still want to know what happens next") is the most generic moment in the chapter—it's the kind of thing any YA novel might say, and it doesn't sound like Nish. The line "which in this shit town isn't worth much anyway" is similarly a cliché.

The tooth detail appears without prior establishment in this chapter, suggesting it was meant to be seeded earlier. As a standalone chapter, it's slightly confusing.

TONAL CONSISTENCY: The balance of comedy and dread is well-maintained. Jordan carrying Keya's bags "like a proper boyfriend which nobody saw coming" is a perfect throwaway line. The mundane (dad arguing about cheese and onion proportions) against the supernatural (Eugenie appearing outside) is handled with skill.

FAITHFULNESS TO PLAN: The chapter follows the plan closely—three weeks later, Nish still has black pudding, Eugenie appears, Felix disappears, and the story ends mid-sentence. The plan mentioned "I think Felix might come back" and the chapter delivers this with Francis. The plan's ending "tell him I've got black pudding in my bag. Just in case he's actually" is echoed in the chapter's structure if not its exact words.

Overall, this is a strong concluding chapter that maintains voice, delivers on the story's promises of non-resolution, and ends effectively. Its weaknesses are in the over-explained emotional beats near the end and one or two generic lines that don't sound like Nish.

chapter_raw_judge_text.7:
[Analysis]

This chapter is a strong execution of the established voice and concept. Let me work through it carefully.

**Voice and Character Consistency**

Nish's voice is remarkably consistent throughout. The chapter opens with "So that was three weeks ago" which immediately signals the confessional, direct-address mode established in the plan. Her characteristic deflections are present: she catalogues other people's drama (Madison's parents, Keya's pregnancy) before getting to the genuinely strange stuff happening to her. The aside about Yasmin and Uber Eats is exactly the kind of casually mean, specific detail that makes Nish feel real rather than constructed. Her self-correction ("I checked, don't judge me") and her dismissal of her brother's lottery ambitions are consistent with the profile's description of someone who notices everything and is casually judgmental.

The moment where she catches herself staring at Tom's jugular is genuinely effective - it's the chapter's best beat, where the horror bleeds into Nish's psychology without being announced. "Very visible. Very accessible." is chilling precisely because Nish doesn't seem to register how chilling it is.

**Plot and Structure**

The chapter largely follows the plan but makes some significant structural changes. The plan had this as Chapter 7 ("Shit Gets Weirder") covering Felix texting sixty times, the fire alarm interruption, and a second blood text from a different number. The actual chapter is more of a synthesis of Chapters 7 and 8 from the plan - it covers Felix's disappearance, the ongoing texts, the burned house, Eugenie in Tesco, and the Birmingham offer. This is a notable deviation from the plan, though the material is handled well.

The Birmingham scholarship/Medicine offer is a smart addition not in the plan - it gives Nish a concrete reason to stay that she's choosing to ignore, which deepens her character. The tooth under the pillow is a good horror beat.

**What Works**

The mundane-horror juxtaposition is consistently effective. Eugenie buying reduced black pudding at Tesco Express while being an ancient vampire is exactly the right register. The corrupted files and the house burning down "quietly without telling anyone" are genuinely unsettling details delivered in Nish's matter-of-fact voice. The texts escalating from emoji to coordinates to "He's so hungry he's forgotten how to be human" is well-paced.

The Keya pregnancy reveal is handled with exactly the right tone - "casual as anything, eating chips like 'yeah so I'm keeping it'" - and Jordan crying into his Subway sandwich is a specific, funny, sad detail.

**What Doesn't Work**

The chapter is somewhat tell-heavy in places. The explanation of what's happening to Nish (the texts, the paintings disappearing, the house burning) is delivered as a list rather than dramatized. This is partly intentional given the confessional format, but it means the chapter lacks scenes - it's mostly summary.

The Birmingham scholarship feels slightly convenient as a plot device and the "exceptional circumstances" explanation is vague in a way that feels like a placeholder rather than deliberate mystery.

The dialogue is minimal and functional rather than distinctive. The exchange with her dad ("Are you selling them?" / "To who? The black pudding dealers?") is good, but there's not much of it.

The ending with the tooth under the pillow is effective but the "just in case" callback is slightly too neat for a story that's supposed to resist resolution.

**Amateurish Elements**

The chapter is not amateurish in execution. The voice is controlled, the details are specific, and the tonal balance between comedy and dread is maintained. There are no purple prose moments - the writing is deliberately flat and colloquial, which serves the piece. No forced metaphors; the imagery that appears (the house as "black bones of building") is brief and earns its place.

The main weakness is structural: this reads more like a penultimate chapter than a middle chapter, and it's doing the work of two planned chapters in one, which means some of the planned beats (Felix cornering her, the fire alarm, the conversation about "the original arrangement") are lost.

final_raw_judge_texts:
[Analysis]

This is a darkly comic British YA piece told in first-person by Nish, a working-class girl from the Midlands who gets drawn into a mystery involving Felix, a posh new boy with apparent vampiric tendencies. The prompt asked for darkly comic British YA fiction, and the piece largely delivers on that brief with considerable skill.

VOICE AND CHARACTER: The narrator's voice is the piece's greatest strength. Nish is genuinely funny, sharp, and specific - her observations about school social dynamics, her dad's Leicester City pajamas, Keya's pregnancy, Jordan's tactical chundering in the koi pond - these feel authentically rendered. The voice is consistent throughout all eight chapters, which is a real achievement. Felix is also well-drawn as a character: his awkwardness, his posh-boy fish-out-of-water quality, his genuine desperation all feel earned. The dynamic between them has real chemistry even without being romantic.

DIALOGUE: The texting exchanges are particularly strong - the rhythm of Felix's formal language against Nish's casual texting style creates genuine comedy. The in-person dialogue is also mostly effective, though it occasionally becomes a vehicle for exposition ("What arrangement?" "Nothing." "Felix." "I can't tell you." etc.) which is a weakness.

PLOT AND STRUCTURE: The mystery unfolds at a reasonable pace through the first five chapters, but then the structure becomes somewhat repetitive. Chapters 6 and 7 both begin with "So that was three weeks ago" which is either a deliberate structural joke or an error - it reads as an error. The plot stalls in the middle chapters with repeated variations of "Felix is hungry / Eugenie is threatening / I don't know what's happening." The introduction of Francis in Chapter 8 is interesting but feels rushed and underdeveloped. The ending is deliberately unresolved (mid-sentence cliffhanger), which is a legitimate choice for YA but feels slightly cheap here.

WORLD BUILDING: The world is built through accumulation of specific detail rather than exposition - the murder house, the paintings, the antique teeth, the black pudding obsession. This is skillfully done. The vampire mythology is deliberately kept vague, which works for the genre and the narrator's perspective.

COMEDY: The dark comedy lands consistently. The observations about school life, class dynamics, and teenage behavior are sharp and specific. The running gag about black pudding as vampire sustenance is genuinely clever - it's both funny and thematically coherent (iron content, blood sausage, etc.). The Keya pregnancy subplot adds texture without overwhelming the main story.

WEAKNESSES: The piece has some structural problems. The repetition in later chapters (multiple "three weeks ago" time jumps, repeated "what arrangement?" exchanges) suggests the author ran out of plot momentum. The Francis introduction in Chapter 8 is intriguing but feels like a new story beginning rather than a satisfying development of the existing one. The piece never quite commits to revealing what Felix is, which is fine, but the hints become repetitive rather than building tension. Some of the "weird things happening" accumulate without escalating - the burning house, the disappearing paintings, the coordinates - these feel like they should add up to something but don't quite cohere. The ending mid-sentence is a bit of a cop-out.

TONE: The balance between comedy and genuine unease is well-maintained for most of the piece. The darkness never overwhelms the comedy, and the comedy never deflates the genuine mystery. This is hard to do and the piece mostly succeeds.

AMATEURISH ELEMENTS: The chapter 7 beginning repeating chapter 6's opening is a notable error. Some of the later chapters feel like they're spinning wheels. The "I'm not going. Obviously I'm not going... But I set an alarm for 11:45" is a well-worn YA trope deployed without subversion.

Overall this is a strong, confident piece of genre writing with a genuinely distinctive voice, some real wit, and effective genre mechanics, held back by structural repetition and an unresolved ending that feels incomplete rather than deliberately open.

chapter_raw_judges_text.2:
[Analysis]

This chapter demonstrates a strong command of the first-person teenage voice the plan calls for. Nish's narration is consistently rendered - the self-interruptions, the casual cruelty, the mercenary logic ("thirty quid for frozen meat is the easiest money I've ever made"), the social anxiety about Madison seeing her by the bins. The voice feels genuinely inhabited rather than performed.

The dialogue is one of the chapter's strongest elements. The exchange about spinach, the "miscommunicated with yourself" bit, the "adjusting to the Midlands" reveal - these land with genuine comic timing. Felix's speech patterns ("Different resources," "She smells like survival") do the work of suggesting something off without overplaying it. The dialogue feels like actual teenagers (and one possibly-not-teenager) rather than a writer's approximation of teenagers.

The chapter follows its plan faithfully: Felix appears looking rough, eats frozen black pudding and cries, explains "dietary restrictions," pays Nish extra for silence, and ends with the ambiguous "she smells like survival" line. The plan's beats are all hit without feeling mechanical.

Character consistency is strong. Felix is catastrophically lonely, apologetic, speaks like he swallowed a dictionary, and his desperation over the black pudding reads as both comic and genuinely sad. Nish is mercenary, judgmental (the "doesn't know where to buy drugs, probably thinks you get them from an actual pharmacy" line), socially anxious about Madison, and ultimately motivated by money rather than kindness. This is exactly the character profile.

The "she smells like survival" line is the chapter's best moment - it's genuinely unsettling, lands the vampire ambiguity perfectly, and is earned by the preceding scene rather than dropped in arbitrarily.

Where the chapter is slightly weaker: the bins-smell opening paragraph is functional but not particularly inspired. The "like when Keya sees there's chocolate cake at lunch" simile is fine but a bit flat. The chapter doesn't quite achieve the "genuinely mean British teenage behavior" the plan promises - Nish is more wryly observational than mean here, which is appropriate for a chapter where she's mostly alone with Felix, but the edge is slightly softer than the plan suggests.

The "true crime podcast" framing at the opening is a nice touch that fits Nish's character (she wants to do true crime podcasts per her profile). The detail about the Year 7 tuna pasta bake is exactly the kind of specific, grounding detail the plan calls for.

There's no purple prose, no forced metaphors, no tell-don't-show problems of note. The writing is lean and purposeful. The darkness (Felix's genuine desperation, the concerning paleness, the crying) is earned by the comic setup rather than imposed on it.

Minor quibble: "Like call-the-school-nurse pale" is slightly clunky as a construction, and the repetition of "complicated" in the middle section is intentional but runs slightly long. These are small issues in an otherwise well-executed chapter.

The rhythm of the texts - dots appearing and disappearing, the cascade of messages - is rendered convincingly.

[Analysis]

This chapter demonstrates a remarkably confident command of voice and character. The first-person narration through Nish is consistently realized - she's judgmental, self-aware in patches, funny in the way she thinks she's funnier than she is, and genuinely observant in ways that feel earned rather than performed. The chapter successfully executes the plan's core intention: making everyone a bit shit while keeping the vampire ambiguity alive.

The dialogue is a genuine strength. The chicken exchange ("But I had food." / "You can't bring outside chicken into the canteen." / "Why?" / "Because you can't.") captures the specific texture of teenage conversation without feeling scripted. Felix's voice is appropriately off - Victorian politeness colliding with modern school life - and the "medicinal black pudding" exchange lands well. Keya's pregnancy reveal is dropped casually mid-paragraph, exactly as Nish would deliver it, which is a nice structural choice. The texting exchange at the end maintains the voice throughout and the "Evidence" folder gag is genuinely funny rather than trying-to-be-funny.

The chapter follows its plan closely: Felix's social incompetence (the chicken, the card system), the Eugenie/panther moment, the "That's just Eugenie hunting" line, and the general escalation of weirdness. The detail about Felix moving "around" Mr. Davies in a physically impossible way is well-placed - specific enough to be unsettling without being heavy-handed.

Where the chapter is slightly weaker: the "impossibly around him. Like when you replay a video and realize the physics don't make sense" moment is the closest thing to a forced metaphor, and it's a bit clunky. The observation about Felix not eating is told more than shown - we're told he never eats normal food rather than seeing it in action. The barbed wire fence moment is good but could be sharper.

The darkness is well-earned - Keya's pregnancy dropped as an aside, Felix's genuine distress about Eugenie, the "something like that" response to "what's it cure, being alive?" - these land because they're not telegraphed. The lightness (the chicken power move, Josh Harlow eating bogies) works because it's grounded in specific, believable teenage logic.

The chapter is coherent, maintains internal consistency, and advances the plot while deepening the mystery without resolving anything. Characters are consistent with their profiles. The voice never slips into adult-writing-as-teenager territory, which is the most common failure mode for this type of fiction.

Minor weaknesses: a couple of moments where the narration tells us how to feel about something rather than letting it land (the "something about the way his face goes even paler (how?) makes me think this is important" is slightly over-explaining). The chapter could be slightly tighter in the middle section. But these are minor complaints against a chapter that largely succeeds at something genuinely difficult.
