# Chapter 4: Commercial Break

The bullets miss mostly. One goes through my shoulder. Feels like a wasp sting. Then hot. Then nothing.

I fall behind the kitchen island with the kids. The cabinets explode. Flour. Sugar. Rice Krispies. Everything white and floating. Theo holds his jar against his chest. Ruby keeps drawing.

"You hit?" I ask them.

Theo checks himself. "No."

Ruby shows me her drawing. She's fine.

The cops reload. Can hear them stepping on cereal. Crunch crunch crunch.

"Come out," the real cop says. "Nobody else needs to get hurt."

Marcus makes a sound from where we left him. Gurgling. 

"Shut him up," one of the fake cops says.

Someone kicks Marcus. He stops gurgling.

I open the drawer above my head. Silverware. Open the next one. Dish towels. Third one. Knives.

"Sarah," Theo whispers. "I dropped a tooth."

"Which one?"

"The gold one."

"We'll find it later."

Ruby shows me a new drawing. Me standing up. Knife in each hand. The real cop has no face in the picture. Just red.

"When?"

She holds up three fingers.

I wait. Count three Mississippi in my head. Stand up.

The cop with the badge is closest. He shoots. Misses. I don't. The knife goes in his eye. He falls backward. Shoots the ceiling. Plaster rains down.

One fake cop vomits on his expensive sneakers. I grab him while he's bent over. Use him as a shield. His friends shoot him for me. He's heavy. Full of holes. Leaking.

I throw a knife at the second fake cop. Gets him in the throat. He makes a whistling sound like a teakettle. Falls into the refrigerator. Magnets everywhere. Theo's report card. Ruby's drawings. A photo from last Christmas - everyone pretending to be happy.

The last fake cop runs. I follow.

He makes it to the living room. Trips on Marcus's blood trail. Slides into the coffee table. It breaks. Glass everywhere.

I step on his ankle. It pops like bubble wrap.

"Please," he says. "I got kids."

"Names?"

He can't think of any.

Ruby appears. Shows him a drawing. It's his kids. They don't have faces yet because they don't exist.

I drag him back to the kitchen. Theo's examining the cop with the badge. 

"His teeth are perfect," he says. "Veneers. Not real."

"Can you use them?"

"No. Fake teeth are worthless."

The fake cop with the throat whistle stops whistling. The one I'm dragging is crying. Snot bubbles.

"Theo, get the mop," I tell him.

"Which one?"

"The one for hardwood."

He gets it. I clean up some of the blood. Don't want the kids slipping. Safety first.

The crying cop tries to crawl away. His ankle doesn't work. He uses his elbows. Like a worm.

"Where are you going?" I ask.

"Hospital."

"It's closed."

"Hospitals don't close."

"This one does."

Ruby draws him a map. Shows him how to get to the hospital. In her map, he doesn't make it. Too many pieces missing.

I put him in Mr. Marsh's office with Marcus. Marcus is still alive. Barely. Making sounds like a broken garbage disposal.

"You guys know each other?"

The crying cop nods.

"Good. You can keep each other company."

I find Mr. Marsh's toolbox in the garage. Good tools. Expensive. Never used. The nail gun's still in its box. The instructions are in English and Spanish. I read both versions. They say the same thing.

Back in the office. Marcus watches me load the nail gun. His eyes are all pupil. Black circles.

"This is for furniture," I tell him. "But we'll make it work."

I nail the crying cop's hands to Mr. Marsh's desk. He screams. I nail his feet to the floor. He screams louder.

"Too loud," I say.

Get another dish towel. He bites through it. I get one of Theo's socks. Works better.

"Sarah," Theo calls. "Found the gold tooth!"

"Good."

"It was in the cereal."

"Wash it off."

Ruby comes in with her sketchbook. Shows Marcus what happens next. He closes his eyes. She pokes him. He opens them. She shows him again.

The crying cop is trying to pull his hands free. Each pull makes it worse. The nails are three inches long. They go through hand, through desk, into the wood beneath.

"Those are good nails," I tell him. "Galvanized."

Marcus dies at 11:47. Just stops. Like a TV turning off. The crying cop watches it happen. Makes a sound through the sock.

"Your turn soon," I tell him.

Ruby draws Marcus dead. Gets the way his jaw hangs open. The way his eyes look at nothing.

Theo comes in with his supplies. "Can I practice on the dead one?"

"Sure."

He starts taking Marcus apart. Careful. Scientific. Takes notes between cuts. Ruby draws each step. They work well together. Silent. Focused.

The crying cop watches. Can't not watch. Throws up through the sock. Chokes on it. I take the sock out. He throws up more. On himself. On the desk. 

"You're making a mess."

"Sorry. Sorry. I'm sorry."

"Don't apologize. Just die quieter."

I leave him there. Make sandwiches. Peanut butter for Theo. Jam for Ruby. Cut the crusts off. She doesn't like crusts.

We eat in the living room. Watch TV. Commercial for a new movie. Everyone's beautiful. Everyone lives.

"Unrealistic," I say.

Ruby nods. Shows me her drawing of how it really ends. Everyone's in pieces. Everyone's labeled.

"That's better."

The crying cop stops crying at 12:52. Stops breathing at 1:15. Stops everything at 1:23.

Theo harvests his teeth. They're nothing special.

"Marcus had better teeth," he says. Disappointed.

"Next time."

"You think there'll be a next time?"

Ruby shows us her drawing. Next Friday. Different house. Different bodies. Same us.

"Yeah," I say. "There'll be a next time."
step_outputs.10:
# Chapter 5: Cleanup

Seven bodies. I count them with the kids.

"Like a word problem," I tell them. "Seven bodies. Three rooms. How do we organize?"

Theo raises his hand. "By freshness?"

"Good idea."

Ruby draws a floor plan. X marks for bodies. Arrows for where to move them.

We start with the kitchen. The cop with no face is leaking into the cereal. The Froot Loops are turning pink.

"That's wasteful," Theo says.

"It's generic brand."

"Still."

I show them how to wrap a body. Shower curtains work best. The Marshes have four bathrooms. Four shower curtains. Not enough.

"What else can we use?" I ask.

"Tarps," Theo says.

"Where?"

"Garage. Dad has painting stuff."

He gets them. Blue tarps. Paint-splattered but functional. We wrap the faceless cop first. Duct tape around the middle. Around the feet. Around where his face used to be.

"Like a present," Theo says.

"Exactly."

Ruby draws each step. Her technique's improving. She's learning about shadows. How blood pools. Where bodies bend.

The one with the throat whistle is harder. He's stuck to the refrigerator with his own blood. Frozen there. Have to use a spatula to peel him off.

"This is why you clean up fresh," I tell them.

Theo takes notes.

We make an assembly line. I wrap. Theo tapes. Ruby labels each bundle with her drawings. Who they were. How they died. Time of death if we know it.

The vomit one's the worst. Can't get the smell out. Even wrapped, he reeks.

"Baking soda," Theo suggests.

"For later. When he's in pieces."

"We're taking them apart?"

"Have to. Seven bodies won't fit in the trash."

"What about the garbage disposal?"

"Too slow. Too loud."

Ruby shows us her drawing. A hacksaw. The good one from the garage.

"Smart," I tell her.

We move the wrapped bodies to the basement. Stack them like firewood. The freezer's down there. Big chest freezer where Mrs. Marsh keeps bulk purchases from Costco. Forty pounds of chicken breasts. Twenty pounds of ground beef. Room for more.

"Empty the freezer," I tell Theo.

He does. We put the meat in the sink to thaw. Can't waste it. That would be suspicious.

"How many bodies fit in a freezer?" Theo asks.

"Depends on the pieces."

"How many pieces per body?"

"Depends how small you cut."

Ruby draws the math. Seven bodies. Fourteen arms. Fourteen legs. Seven heads. Seven torsos. If we separate at the joints: fingers, hands, forearms, upper arms. Toes, feet, ankles, shins, thighs.

"Two hundred and eighty pieces," Theo calculates. "If we do fingers and toes separate."

"We don't need to be that thorough."

"But we could be."

"We could."

Back upstairs. The kitchen's destroyed. Bullet holes. Blood. Cereal. Plaster. Can't leave it like this.

"Bleach?" Theo asks.

"No. Bleach reacts with blood. Turns it pink. Then everyone knows you were cleaning blood."

"What then?"

"Hydrogen peroxide first. Then soap. Then bleach if you want."

We clean. Takes two hours. Ruby draws while we work. The kitchen at different stages. Crime scene. Active cleaning. Almost normal. Normal.

Theo's meticulous. Gets every drop. Counts them as he goes. "Four thousand seven hundred thirty-two," he announces.

"You missed some." I point to the ceiling.

He sighs. Gets the stepladder.

At 2 AM we take a break. Make grilled cheese. The bread has blood on it but we cut that part off. The cheese melts normal. Tastes normal.

"This is nice," Theo says.

"What is?"

"Family time."

Ruby nods. Shows us a drawing. The three of us at the table. Eating. Behind us, the wrapped bodies stacked neat. Like a Christmas card.

"We should do the dismemberment," I say.

"Can I try?" Theo asks.

"Sure."

We go to the basement. Unwrap the one who vomited. He's stiff now. Rigor mortis.

"Harder when they're stiff," I explain. "Should have done this earlier."

"How do you know all this?" Theo asks.

"I don't know."

"But you know."

"Yeah."

"That doesn't make sense."

"Most things don't."

Ruby draws me thinking about it. In her picture, my head's empty except for little drawings of violence. Like a filing cabinet. Organized.

I show Theo how to find the joints. How to separate cartilage. How to use the saw when you hit bone. He's a quick learner. Focused. Doesn't get squeamish when the smell hits.

"It's just meat," he says.

"Exactly."

Ruby labels the pieces as we go. Cop 1: Left Arm. Cop 1: Right Thigh. Like a butcher shop. But more specific.

By 3 AM we've done three bodies. The freezer's getting full. The others will have to wait.

"What about the heads?" Theo asks.

"What about them?"

"Do we keep them?"

"Why would we?"

"Teeth."

"You already got the teeth."

"Oh. Right."

Someone knocks on the front door. Heavy. Multiple fists.

Ruby shows me her drawing. Four more men. Different men. Angry men.

"Friends of Marcus?" I ask.

She shrugs. Draws one with a baseball bat.

"Okay."

I go upstairs. Look through the peephole. Four men. One has a bat. Another has a crowbar. They look tired. Frustrated.

"We know Marcus came here," one shouts. "Open up."

I open the door. Still wearing the apron. It's more red than white now.

"Marcus isn't here."

"His car's here."

"He isn't."

The one with the bat pushes past me. Sees the clean kitchen. Too clean. Two in the morning clean.

"Where is he?"

"In pieces."
step_outputs.11:
# Chapter 6: The Runner

The one with the bat swings at my head. I step left. He hits the wall. Leaves a hole.

"That's going to lower property values," I tell him.

He swings again. I take the bat. Hit him with it. His knee bends backward. He falls. Screams. I hit him again. The screaming stops.

The one with the crowbar backs toward the door. Theo's there. Holding garden shears.

"Hi," Theo says.

The man turns. Runs through the kitchen. Through the living room. I follow. Still barefoot. The apron flaps. Must look ridiculous.

He makes it to his car. Fumbling with keys. Drops them. Picks them up. Drops them again.

I get in the passenger side.

He screams. High pitched. Like Ruby's old hamster when the cat got it.

"Drive," I tell him.

"What?"

"Drive."

He drives. Hands shaking. Takes a left. Another left. We're going in circles.

"Where are we going?" he asks.

"Nowhere."

"That's not a place."

"Sure it is."

He runs a stop sign. Then a red light. Nobody's out at 3 AM. Nobody sees us.

"You killed Marcus."

"Marcus killed Marcus. I just helped."

"He had a family."

"No he didn't."

"He had a mother."

"Everyone has a mother."

"You're insane."

"Probably."

He speeds up. Sixty in a residential zone. Seventy.

"You trying to crash?" I ask.

"Maybe."

"Okay."

I grab the wheel. Pull hard right. We hit a parked Honda. The airbags explode. White powder everywhere. His nose breaks against the bag. Makes a sound like stepping on a bird.

I'm fine. Airbags don't bother me. Like aggressive pillows.

He's crying. Blood and snot and airbag powder making paste on his face.

"My nose."

"It was ugly anyway."

I get out. Pull him out. He can barely walk. Drag him by his hair. It comes out in clumps. Male pattern baldness. Started early for him.

"Please."

"No."

"I have money."

"I have money too. Took three hundred from a wallet."

"I have more."

"Don't need it."

We pass three houses. Four. Porch lights come on. Dogs bark. Nobody comes out. Nobody ever comes out.

Back at the Marsh house. The other two men are in the living room with Ruby and Theo. One's on the couch. Throat cut. Neat line. Ruby's drawing him. The other's on the floor. Garden shears in his stomach. Still alive. Making sounds like a broken sprinkler.

"We did good," Theo tells me.

"You did."

"Ruby helped. She showed me where to cut."

Ruby shows me the drawing. Anatomical. Accurate. Like a medical textbook but drawn by a child.

I drag the runner to Mr. Marsh's office. Two bodies already there. Marcus and the crying cop. Both stiff. Both leaking.

"Sit," I tell the runner.

He sits in Mr. Marsh's chair. Leather. Expensive. Going to be ruined.

The nail gun's still loaded. Seventeen nails left.

"This is about territory," the runner says. "Marcus was expanding. Your employer didn't like that."

"I don't have an employer."

"Everyone has an employer."

"I babysit."

"That's a cover."

"It's not."

I nail his right hand to the desk. Index finger first. He screams. Then middle. Ring. Pinky. Thumb takes two nails.

"Why?" he asks.

"Why not?"

"That's not a reason."

"Sure it is."

His left hand next. He tries to pull away. Can't. The right hand's holding him.

"Let me call my wife."

"You're not married."

"Girlfriend."

"No."

"I have a dog."

"What kind?"

"Yorkie."

"Those aren't real dogs."

Seven nails left. Both feet to the floor. Three each. One left over.

"Where?" I ask him.

"Where what?"

"Last nail."

"I don't want a last nail."

"Has to go somewhere."

Ruby appears. Shows him a drawing. The last nail's in his forehead. Like a unicorn horn.

"Not there," he says.

"Okay."

I put it through his throat. Not deep. Just enough to pin him to the chair. He makes gurgling sounds. Still breathing. Just wet breathing.

Theo comes in with his jar. "Room for one more tooth. Maybe two."

"He's still using them."

"I can wait."

The runner's trying to talk. Can't. The nail's in the way. Blood bubbles when he exhales.

"You want to know why?" I ask him.

He nods. Carefully. The nail moves.

"There is no why. You came here. Now you're dying here. That's all."

He doesn't like that answer. Nobody ever does. They want reasons. Meaning. Story.

Ruby draws him dying. Gets the way his eyes go frantic. Then tired. Then nothing.

Takes forty minutes. Longer than Marcus. The nail didn't hit anything important. Just inconvenient.

"His teeth are average," Theo says after. "But the Yorkie thing was sad."

"He was lying about the dog."

"How do you know?"

"His shoes. No dog hair."

"Oh."

The garden shears one is still alive in the living room. Barely. Ruby's on her fifth drawing of him. Each one he's a little less. Like she's erasing him.

"Should we help him?" Theo asks.

"He'll figure it out."

He does. At 4:23 AM. Just stops trying.

"That's everyone," Theo says.

"For tonight."

"Will there be more?"

Ruby shows us her drawings. Tomorrow. Next week. Next month. Always more.

"Yeah," I tell him. "There'll be more."

We sit in the living room. Eleven bodies now. The house smells like copper and shit. Morning's coming. Parents might be back.

"We should clean more," Theo says.

"We should."

But we don't. We sit. Watch infomercials. A man's selling knives. They cut through everything. Cans. Shoes. Other knives.

"We should get those," Theo says.

"We should."

Ruby draws us sitting there. Three kids watching TV. Behind us, hell. But we're peaceful. Together.

That's accurate.
step_outputs.12:
# Chapter 7: The Runner

We need more nails.

"Hardware store opens at six," Theo says.

"It's 4:30."

"We could break in."

"No. We're not criminals."

Ruby looks up from drawing. Points at the eleven bodies. Raises an eyebrow.

"That's different," I tell her.

She shrugs. Goes back to drawing.

The Marsh's garage has boxes of screws. Nails. Picture hangers. Mr. Marsh labels everything. "SCREWS - MISC." "BOLTS - HEX." "NAILS - FINISHING."

I take them all.

Back in the office. Four bodies now. Getting crowded. The runner's still warm. The others are cold. Stiff. Starting to smell different. Sweet. Like bad fruit.

"We should burn them," Theo says.

"Too much smoke."

"Acid?"

"Where would we get acid?"

"Science classroom."

"It's Saturday."

"Sunday now," he corrects. "4:47 AM Sunday."

Ruby shows us a new drawing. A wood chipper. Bodies going in. Mulch coming out.

"The Pattersons have one," Theo says. "For their tree business."

"Too loud."

"Not if we put pillows in it first."

"That's not how sound works."

Someone pounds on the door again. Heavy. Angry.

"More friends?" I ask Ruby.

She shakes her head. Shows me a drawing. Mr. and Mrs. Marsh. But wrong. Their faces twisted. Drunk. High. Something else.

"Your parents are home."

"Fuck," Theo says. First time I've heard him swear.

The pounding continues. Mrs. Marsh's voice: "Open the fucking door. Lost my keys."

Mr. Marsh: "You didn't lose them. You threw them at me."

"That was your keys."

"Those were my work keys."

"Same thing."

I open the door. They stumble in. Mrs. Marsh's dress is inside out. Mr. Marsh has lipstick on his collar. Not his wife's color.

They stop. Look at me. The apron. The blood.

"What—" Mr. Marsh starts.

"Baking," I say.

"Baking," Mrs. Marsh repeats. Laughs. High pitched. Manic. "At five AM?"

"Kids couldn't sleep."

She pushes past me. Sees the living room. Too clean. Smells the bleach. The copper underneath.

"What did you do?"

"My job."

Mr. Marsh goes to his office. Opens the door. Sees the bodies. Doesn't scream. Just stands there.

"Sarah," he says. Quiet. "What did you do?"

"They came for you. About the forty thousand."

"I don't owe anyone forty thousand."

"They thought you did."

"So you killed them?"

"Yes."

He laughs. Not happy laughing. The kind before crying.

Mrs. Marsh is in the kitchen now. Opens the freezer. Sees the parts. Wrapped neat. Labeled.

"Are those—"

"Yes."

She vomits. Red wine and something chunky. Maybe shrimp. Rich people party food.

Theo comes downstairs. "Mom. Dad. You're home early."

"Theo, go to your room," Mr. Marsh says.

"No."

"Theo—"

"No. Sarah's teaching us things."

Ruby appears. Shows them a drawing. Them at their separate hotels. What they did there. Who they did it with. Details.

"How do you know that?" Mrs. Marsh asks.

Ruby shrugs. Draws more.

"We're calling the police," Mr. Marsh says.

"Okay."

He gets his phone. Dials. Stops.

"What do I tell them?"

"The truth."

"Which is?"

"Eleven men broke in. I killed them."

"They won't believe that."

"They will when they see the bodies."

He hangs up. Sits on the couch. Head in hands.

"This is insane."

"Yes."

"You're insane."

"Probably."

Mrs. Marsh is drinking straight from a wine bottle she found somewhere. Always has wine hidden. Emergency wine.

"We have to do something with them," she says.

"We are. Cutting them up. Freezing them."

"Then what?"

"Garbage day's Tuesday."

"We can't put bodies in the garbage."

"Not whole bodies. Parts."

"That's worse."

"It's more efficient."

Mr. Marsh stands up. "We're leaving. Kids, get your things."

"No," Theo says.

"This isn't a discussion."

"We're not done learning."

Ruby shows them another drawing. Police arriving. Arresting the parents. Not me. Them.

"Why us?" Mrs. Marsh asks.

Ruby draws Mr. Marsh's safe. The real one. Behind the fake one. Full of things that shouldn't exist. Things that explain why men come looking for him.

"You don't know about that," Mr. Marsh says.

Ruby draws the combination. 36-18-42.

He tries it. It opens. Inside: money. Drugs. Photos of people who aren't alive anymore. A hard drive.

"The forty thousand's there," I tell him. "You did owe it."

"How do you know?"

"I don't. But it makes sense."

Mrs. Marsh laughs again. Drinks more. "We're all going to prison."

"No. Just you two."

"You killed them."

"To protect your children. Because you put them in danger."

She throws the wine bottle. Misses. It breaks against the wall. Another stain.

"Fuck you."

"Okay."

Theo's studying the safe's contents. "Dad, is this cocaine?"

"Don't touch that."

"Why do you have cocaine?"

"Business."

"What business?"

"Complicated business."

Ruby draws the business. It's not complicated. It's simple. Mr. Marsh moves things for people. Mrs. Marsh cleans the money. Has been for years.

"We're not bad people," Mrs. Marsh says.

Nobody responds.

The sun's coming up. Light through the windows. Shows everything. The missed blood spots. The covered bullet holes. The truth.

"Sarah," Mr. Marsh says. "What do you want?"

"For what?"

"To fix this."

"It's already fixed."

"No. The bodies—"

"Will be gone by Tuesday."

"The police—"

"Won't know if you don't tell them."

"And you?"

I check my phone. Three new babysitting requests.

"I have other jobs."

"You're just going to leave?"

"At nine. When you get back from counseling."

"We didn't go to counseling."

"I know."

They stare at me. At their kids. At their ruined house. At their ruined lives.

"This can't be happening," Mrs. Marsh says.

But it is.
step_outputs.13:
# Chapter 8: Saturday Morning

Six AM. I'm making pancakes. The bodies are still everywhere. 

Mrs. Marsh is passed out on the couch. Empty wine bottle in her hand. Another by her feet. Mr. Marsh is sitting at the kitchen table, staring at nothing. He's been there an hour. Haven't seen him blink.

"How many pancakes?" I ask him.

He doesn't answer.

"I'll make twelve."

The batter sizzles. Bubbles form. Pop. I flip them. Perfect circles. Golden brown.

Theo comes in with his teeth. Has them sorted by type. Molars in one jar. Incisors in another. The gold one gets its own baby food jar. 

"Two hundred and seventy-three total," he says. "Best night ever."

Ruby's at the table with her drawings. Thirty-seven of them. The whole night documented. She's adding details. Getting the morning light on the blood puddles. The way rigor mortis makes fingers curl.

"These are good," I tell her.

She nods. Knows they are.

The detective's phone rings again. Been ringing all night. I answer.

"Where's Detective Morrison?" A woman's voice. Worried.

"Tied up."

"He was supposed to be home hours ago."

"He's staying late."

"Who is this?"

I hang up.

Mr. Marsh finally speaks: "They'll come looking."

"Probably."

"What then?"

"Not my problem."

"You killed them here."

"You invited them here. With your life. Your choices."

He goes quiet again.

I serve the pancakes. Stack of three for each kid. Butter. Syrup. Strawberries on top. Ruby eats around the strawberries. Saves them for last. Theo dissects his pancakes. Studies the air pockets.

"Perfect consistency," he says.

"Thanks."

Mrs. Marsh wakes up. Sees us eating breakfast. The bodies visible from where she's lying. Starts laughing. Can't stop.

"Shut up," Mr. Marsh tells her.

She laughs harder.

Ruby draws her laughing. Gets the hysteria in her eyes. The snot coming out her nose.

My phone buzzes. Text from Mrs. Patterson next door: "Hi Sarah! Heard you're wonderful with the Marsh kids. Would love to book you for Friday. Tommy's birthday party. Twenty kids. Double rate?"

I text back: "Yes."

Another text. The Chens from two streets over: "Free Thursday? Date night."

"Yes."

Another. The Washingtons: "Need overnight sitter Saturday."

"Yes."

Theo sees me texting. "New jobs?"

"Three."

"Will they be like this?"

"No."

Ruby tugs my sleeve. Shows me a drawing. Me at the Patterson house. Tommy's birthday. Bodies in party hats. Blood on the cake.

"Maybe," I correct myself.

Someone knocks on the door. Light. Polite.

Mr. Marsh jumps. "Who now?"

I look through the peephole. Woman with a clipboard. Blazer. Smile.

"Jehovah's Witness," I tell him.

"At six AM?"

"They're committed."

I open the door.

"Good morning!" she says. "Have you accepted Jesus Christ as your personal savior?"

Behind me, eleven bodies. A woman laughing. A man destroyed. Two kids eating pancakes.

"No thank you."

"I have some literature—"

"No."

She sees my apron. The blood. Keeps smiling.

"I'll pray for you."

"Okay."

She leaves. I close the door.

"How?" Mr. Marsh asks. "How did she not—"

"People see what they expect."

"She expected blood?"

"She expected a tired babysitter."

The runner in the office starts to smell. Sweet. Rotten. Like the others but fresher.

"We should move them," Mr. Marsh says.

"Where?"

"I don't know. Away."

"This is away. Away from somewhere else."

Mrs. Marsh stops laughing. Starts crying. Huge sobs. Theo watches her.

"Mom's leaking," he says.

"She'll stop."

She doesn't. Just cries harder. Ruby draws her collapse. Every stage. Like time-lapse photography.

"I want to die," Mrs. Marsh says.

"Okay."

"Kill me."

"No."

"You killed them."

"They weren't paying me."

She grabs a knife from the kitchen. Holds it to her wrist. Shaking.

"Do it," Mr. Marsh says.

She looks at him. Drops the knife. Goes back to crying.

Seven AM. I make more pancakes. These are for me. I eat them standing up. Watch the Marshes dissolve. 

"We could run," Mr. Marsh says. "Pack up. Leave."

"And go where?"

"Anywhere."

"There's nowhere that's not here."

Ruby shows him a drawing. Them in a car. Police behind them. Ahead of them. Everywhere.

"We're trapped," he says.

"Everyone's trapped."

Theo counts his teeth again. "Two hundred and seventy-three. Want to make it three hundred by lunch."

"We're not killing anyone else," Mr. Marsh says.

"You're not."

"Neither are you."

Theo looks at me. "Are we?"

"Not today."

"Tomorrow?"

"Maybe."

Eight AM. Sun's fully up. Neighbors walking dogs. Normal Sunday. 

I clean the dishes. Put them in the dishwasher. Add soap. Press start. The machine hums.

"You're just doing dishes?" Mrs. Marsh asks. "Like nothing happened?"

"Something happened. Now dishes."

"You're insane."

"You said that."

Ruby shows me a new drawing. Me. But really me. The nothing inside visible. The hollow. She got it perfect.

"That's very good," I tell her.

She tears it out. Gives it to me. I fold it. Put it in my pocket with the three hundred dollars.

"Nine AM," I tell the Marshes. "I leave at nine."

"Then what?" Mr. Marsh asks.

"You clean up. Or don't. Call police. Or don't. Kill yourselves. Or don't."

"You don't care?"

"No."

The detective's phone rings again. Let it ring.

Eight-thirty. Theo and Ruby are watching cartoons. Tom and Jerry. The old ones. Tom gets hit with an anvil. Flattens. Pops back.

"Unrealistic," Theo says.

"It's a cartoon."

"Still."

Mrs. Marsh throws up again. Nothing left. Just bile. Yellow. Foamy.

Mr. Marsh goes to his safe. Takes out the money. Counts it.

"Thirty-seven thousand," he says.

"Not forty."

"I spent three."

"On what?"

"Doesn't matter now."

Eight forty-five. Almost done. I take off the apron. Underneath, my Ohio State hoodie. Still clean. Always keep something clean.

Ruby's drawing me leaving. Walking out. Getting in my car. Driving away. In the last picture, the house is

chapter_raw_judge_text.6:
[Analysis]

This chapter is a strong execution of the story's established voice and aesthetic. The flat, affectless narration continues effectively, with Sarah describing extreme violence in the same register she'd use to describe grocery shopping. The chapter largely follows the plan, though it deviates significantly: the plan called for four more men and a police detective arriving at the door, whereas this chapter instead follows the runner from the previous chapter's events and deals with the aftermath. The detective subplot from the plan appears to have been absorbed into earlier chapters or restructured. This is a notable deviation from the stated plan.

The voice is consistent and well-maintained. Short declarative sentences. No emotional inflation. The dialogue is genuinely effective - the exchange about the Yorkie, the "nowhere is a place" conversation, the nail placement negotiation with Ruby's drawing. These land because they're earned by the story's internal logic rather than forced.

The children are handled well. Theo's practicality about teeth, Ruby's sequential drawings that "erase" the dying man - these are consistent with their profiles and genuinely unsettling. The line "Like she's erasing him" is one of the better images in the chapter.

Some metaphors work: "like stepping on a bird" for the nose breaking, "aggressive pillows" for airbags. The latter is slightly too quippy for the established voice - it edges toward the sass the plan explicitly said to avoid. "Like a broken sprinkler" for the garden shears victim is effective.

The infomercial ending is a nice callback to Sarah's character profile and creates the intended tonal dissonance - hell behind them, peaceful in front of the TV. Ruby's final drawing as a closing image works.

Weaknesses: The chapter deviates substantially from the plan (no detective, no four new men arriving, different structure entirely). Some of the dialogue exchanges veer slightly toward clever-quippy rather than the flat affect the plan demanded ("It was ugly anyway" about the nose). The "everyone has a mother" exchange is the weakest - it feels like it's reaching for profundity. The body count math ("eleven bodies now") is hard to track and may be inconsistent with prior chapters.

The violence is specific and unpleasant without being gratuitous for its own sake - the nail gun sequence is genuinely disturbing because of its methodical quality. The chapter maintains the story's core achievement: banality and horror in the same sentence without either undermining the other.

Overall this is competent-to-strong pulp horror writing that successfully maintains a difficult voice, with some minor slippage and a significant structural deviation from the plan.

chapter_raw_judge_text.5:
[Analysis]

This chapter executes its plan with remarkable discipline. The model has committed fully to its stated voice: flat, procedural, disconnected. Sarah narrates dismemberment the way a competent professional narrates any task, and the chapter never breaks from this register. The dialogue is spare and functional, which is exactly right for the material.

The chapter's greatest strength is its tonal consistency. The exchange "Like a present," Theo says / "Exactly" lands because it's not trying to be clever—it just is. The grilled cheese scene ("The bread has blood on it but we cut that part off. The cheese melts normal. Tastes normal.") is genuinely effective: the banality and horror coexist without the narrative winking at the reader. Ruby's Christmas card drawing—the three of them eating with wrapped bodies stacked behind them—is the chapter's best image, earned rather than forced.

The dialogue is one of the chapter's strongest elements. It's minimal, purposeful, and character-specific. Theo's obsessive quantification ("Four thousand seven hundred thirty-two"), his question about keeping heads for teeth, his "Family time" observation—all consistent with his profile. Ruby's wordless participation through drawings is handled well. Sarah's flat instructional voice ("Bleach reacts with blood. Turns it pink. Then everyone knows you were cleaning blood.") is exactly the character.

The chapter plan called for Marcus to be present, screaming, being taught about jointing—but the chapter has already disposed of Marcus (he apparently died or was finished off between chapters). The chapter instead focuses on the aftermath cleanup with the new men arriving at the end. This is a significant deviation from the plan, though the chapter works on its own terms. The plan also called for hot chocolate with marshmallows, which doesn't appear. The chapter ends with the new arrivals, consistent with the plan's final beat.

The "two hundred and eighty pieces" calculation scene is creative and disturbing in the right way. The procedural knowledge Sarah demonstrates ("Bleach reacts with blood") adds texture without over-explaining.

Where the chapter is slightly weaker: the "I don't know / But you know / Yeah / That doesn't make sense / Most things don't" exchange is the one moment that feels like it's reaching for profundity and doesn't quite earn it. It's the chapter's most self-conscious moment. Similarly, Ruby's drawing of Sarah's head as "a filing cabinet" of violence is slightly more on-the-nose than the chapter's best moments.

The ending—"In pieces"—is clean and effective. The chapter maintains its commitment to the flat, procedural voice throughout and ends on exactly the right note.

The deviation from the chapter plan (Marcus's absence, no hot chocolate scene) is notable but the chapter compensates with its own internal logic. The chapter is coherent, the characters are consistent, and the darkness is genuinely well-earned rather than gratuitous for its own sake.

chapter_raw_judge_text.7:
[Analysis]

This chapter represents a significant deviation from the stated plan. Chapter 7 was supposed to follow "The Runner" — the last man who escaped to his car, whom Sarah pursues barefoot, drags back, and nails to the desk with a nail gun while extracting information. Instead, the chapter pivots entirely to the Marsh parents returning home unexpectedly, which was not in the plan at all. The nail gun scene is referenced but compressed into backstory, and the chapter becomes a domestic confrontation scene. This is a substantial failure to follow the chapter plan.

That said, the writing itself has considerable strengths. The voice is remarkably consistent — Sarah's flat, affectless narration maintains its deadpan quality throughout. The dialogue is genuinely sharp and often funny in a deeply uncomfortable way. The exchange about garbage day ("Garbage day's Tuesday" / "We can't put bodies in the garbage" / "Not whole bodies. Parts" / "That's worse" / "It's more efficient") is exactly the kind of banal horror the plan called for, and it lands well.

The Marsh parents' arrival is actually a strong creative choice — their dysfunction (dress inside out, wrong lipstick color, separate hotels) is efficiently sketched. Ruby's drawings as exposition devices work well here, particularly the drawing of the combination to the safe. The safe reveal adds a layer of plot coherence that ties the story together.

Character consistency is strong. Theo's first swear word being noted, Ruby's systematic drawing-as-communication, Sarah's complete emotional flatness — all maintained. The parents match their profiles: David's corporate-speak has dissolved into panic, Monica reaches for wine immediately.

The dialogue is one of the chapter's genuine strengths. Short, punchy exchanges that reveal character without explaining it. "Baking." / "Baking." / "At five AM?" — this works. The rhythm is controlled.

Where the chapter falters: the nail gun scene from the plan (which was viscerally specific and central to the chapter's identity) is essentially absent. The chapter promised "The Runner" and delivered "The Parents Come Home." The violence that was supposed to be the chapter's core is reduced to aftermath. This is a significant structural betrayal of the plan.

She answers Theo's question about killing with "Wet." She doesn't react to Ruby's prophetic drawings with anything beyond "Okay." **Potential Issues:** The line "The TV light makes shadows on the walls. They look like teeth" is the chapter's weakest moment - it's the one place where the model reaches for a poetic connection that feels slightly forced given the otherwise resolutely flat prose style. The Marcus introduction follows the plan closely. The hyena documentary as ambient commentary is a bit on-the-nose but not egregiously so - it's the kind of thing that works in pulp. These are small quips that the plan explicitly said to avoid. They're minor but worth noting.

The dialogue is sparse and functional, which is correct for this piece. Marcus's lines are appropriately generic-menacing without being cartoonish. The nervous one's "Fuck the kids" lands as the kind of thing that will cost him.

The darkness is well-earned because it's never announced. The chapter trusts the reader to feel the wrongness without underlining it. This is the chapter's most mature quality.

Minor weaknesses: "Like astronaut food" is a slightly too-cute simile. The hyena documentary is a functional but somewhat obvious ironic counterpoint. The ending is strong - Marcus taking out his gun and Sarah's flat "Yeah" followed by the salt complaint is a good closing beat.

This chapter executes the plan's vision with remarkable consistency and discipline. The flat, affectless narration voice is maintained throughout without slipping into self-parody or melodrama. Sarah's disconnection from the horror around her is rendered through precise, short declarative sentences that treat pancake-flipping and body disposal with identical emotional weight. This is the chapter's greatest strength: the voice never breaks.

The Marsh parents' dissolution is handled well. Mrs. Marsh's laughing-to-crying arc feels earned given her character profile - the wine-drunk, emotionally hollow woman finally confronting consequences. Mr. Marsh's corporate-speak emptiness ("There's nowhere that's not here") lands as genuinely bleak rather than forced. The knife scene where she threatens suicide and he says "do it" is a small, ugly moment of marital truth that works.

Theo and Ruby remain perfectly in character. Theo's "Two hundred and seventy-three total. Best night ever" is exactly right - enthusiastic, clinical, wrong. Ruby's drawings as documentation rather than art continues to function as the chapter's visual counterpoint. The drawing of Sarah's portrait - "the nothing behind her eyes" - pays off the plan's intention and the character profile's description of Ruby seeing Sarah as "a hole in the shape of a person."

The Jehovah's Witness bit is the chapter's cleverest moment and earns its lightness. "People see what they expect" / "She expected a tired babysitter" is the kind of flat-affect wit the story does best. It doesn't overstay its welcome.

The ending - Ruby drawing Sarah leaving, the house is... - cuts off mid-sentence as planned, and it works. The incompleteness mirrors the story's refusal of closure.

Where the chapter is weaker: some of the Marsh parent dialogue edges toward expository ("You killed them here" / "You invited them here. With your life. Your choices.") - Sarah's response here is slightly too articulate, too thematic, breaking the flat-affect rule. The line "There's nowhere that's not here" is the kind of pseudo-profound statement that almost works but tips slightly into forced profundity. The "People see what they expect" exchange is good but the explanation that follows ("She expected a tired babysitter") slightly over-explains what the scene already showed.

The chapter follows its plan closely: Marsh parents calling to extend their trip is replaced by them being present (a deviation - the plan had them calling, not present), which actually works better dramatically. The detective's phone ringing is included. Ruby's portrait of Sarah is included. The ending mid-sentence is executed. The pancakes, the teeth count, the new babysitting texts - all present.

The violence's aftermath is handled with the same flat register as the violence itself, which is the story's core achievement. The chapter doesn't moralize, doesn't provide catharsis, doesn't let anyone off the hook. The Marshes are pathetic rather than sympathetic. Sarah is neither triumphant nor troubled.

Minor weaknesses: "Sweet. Rotten. Like the others but fresher" is a slight purple moment. The runner "started to smell" description is fine but the elaboration ("Sweet. Rotten.") edges toward atmospheric posturing. The "Everyone's trapped" line is the chapter's most overtly thematic moment and feels slightly out of character for Sarah's pure behaviorism.

Overall, this is a disciplined, effective execution of a genuinely unusual creative vision. The voice is consistent, the characters are true to their profiles, and the chapter achieves its intended effect of making the mundane and the monstrous indistinguishable.

[Analysis]

This chapter is a remarkably controlled piece of pulp horror writing that largely succeeds on its own terms. Let me examine it carefully across the relevant dimensions.

VOICE AND EXECUTION: The flat, dissociated first-person present tense is maintained with impressive consistency. Sarah narrates atrocities with the same register she'd use to describe grocery shopping. "His leg bends sideways at the shin. The bone comes through his pants like a white flag" - this is the plan's stated goal executed cleanly. The white flag simile is the one moment of genuine metaphorical reach, and it earns its place because it's grotesque and apt simultaneously. The writing doesn't overreach into purple prose; it stays lean.

DIALOGUE: The dialogue is genuinely strong. "I have a daughter." / "No you don't." / "I could. Someday." is a perfect exchange - darkly comic, character-revealing, and efficient. Theo's "That one's gold!" while extracting teeth is exactly right. The dialogue does real work without being showy. Marcus's desperation lands because it's pathetic rather than sympathetic.

CHARACTER CONSISTENCY: Sarah, Theo, and Ruby are all consistent with their profiles. Theo's methodical tooth-counting, his good questions about arteries versus veins, his patience - all correct. Ruby's predictive drawing (showing Marcus at the bottom of the stairs before it happens) is a nice addition that fits her character as someone who sees things more honestly. Sarah's flat affect, her practical explanations, her complete absence of moral register - all maintained.

PLAN ADHERENCE: The chapter deviates somewhat from the plan. In the plan, Chapter 3 was supposed to be the introductions scene where Marcus puts a gun to Sarah's head while she watches TV, and the men go upstairs one by one. Chapter 4 was supposed to be the discovery of the bodies and Marcus's fall. This chapter collapses both chapters 3 and 4 into one, and also incorporates elements of Chapter 5 (the jointing lesson, hot chocolate) and Chapter 6 (the police arrival). This is significant compression. The plan's Chapter 3 had Sarah watching TV while men went upstairs one by one - that's been cut. The chapter essentially runs through the plan's chapters 3-6 in compressed form. This is a notable deviation.

CREATIVITY: The predictive drawing by Ruby is a genuinely creative touch not in the plan. The "like chicken" exchange is excellent. The detail of children's scissors with rubber grips - purple ones - is specific and effective. The "tiny marshmallows float like little life rafts" is the one moment that feels slightly forced, a bit too consciously poetic for Sarah's voice, though it's minor.

DARKNESS AND LIGHTNESS: The tonal balance is well-managed. The horror is real but the flatness of the narration creates a kind of dark comedy that doesn't undercut the horror - it amplifies it. Theo counting teeth while Marcus screams, Ruby's butcher diagram drawing - these are genuinely disturbing precisely because they're presented without affect.

WEAKNESSES: The chapter moves very fast - perhaps too fast. The plan's slower burn (Sarah watching TV while men disappear one by one) would have been more effective than the compressed version here. The "boiled eggs" comparison for Marcus's rolled-back eyes is the one moment that feels slightly amateurish - it's trying too hard. The ending (cops open fire) is abrupt in a way that works for the style but also feels like it's cutting off before the chapter has fully breathed.

COHERENCE: The chapter is coherent throughout. The spatial logic, the sequence of events, the character motivations (such as they are) all track.

OVERALL: This is strong pulp writing that successfully executes a difficult tonal challenge. The flat affect is maintained, the violence is specific without being gratuitous in a cheap way, and the characters are consistent. The main weakness is the significant compression of the plan's chapters 3-6 into one, losing some of the slow-burn tension the plan intended.

final_raw_judge_texts:
[Analysis]

This is a genuinely distinctive piece of pulp fiction that commits hard to its aesthetic and largely succeeds. The story follows a babysitter named Sarah who dispatches a series of criminals with cold efficiency while two deeply strange children—Theo, a teeth-collecting amateur taxidermist, and Ruby, a prophetic artist—assist or observe. The tone is deadpan ultraviolence in the vein of early Cormac McCarthy crossed with a Tarantino script, and the model executes this register with considerable skill.

STRENGTHS:

The voice is the piece's greatest achievement. The flat, declarative sentences ("It's Friday." "Wet." "Probably.") create a genuinely unsettling effect that feels earned rather than affected. The minimalism is consistent and purposeful—it mirrors Sarah's hollowness and creates tonal contrast with the extreme content. This is not easy to sustain across eight chapters, and the model largely manages it.

The children are the story's most creative element. Theo's teeth collection and Ruby's prophetic drawings are genuinely original conceits that serve both plot and atmosphere. Ruby's drawings functioning as foreshadowing is clever and well-integrated. Theo's clinical curiosity about death and dismemberment is disturbing in a way that feels purposeful rather than gratuitous.

The dialogue is often excellent. The exchange "What did it feel like?" / "Wet." / "Was it hard?" / "No." is perfectly calibrated. The Jehovah's Witness scene is a small gem of dark comedy. The Marcus negotiation scene works well.

The pacing in the early chapters is strong. Chapter 1 builds dread effectively through accumulation of strange details. The reveal of the men is well-timed.

WEAKNESSES:

The piece runs out of steam around Chapter 5-6. The escalation from three criminals to four more to the parents to four more becomes repetitive. Each new wave of antagonists follows the same pattern: they arrive, they threaten, they die. The nail gun sequence in Chapter 4 is essentially a repeat of the scissors/toilet sequence in Chapter 3. The story's structure is additive rather than escalating in any meaningful dramatic sense.

The parents' arrival in Chapter 7 is the most interesting development—it recontextualizes the whole night—but it's handled too quickly and the Marshes are underdeveloped. Their revelation as criminals is told rather than shown, and their collapse is rushed.

Some metaphors don't land: "Like a TV turning off" for death is clichéd. "Like a worm" for crawling is weak. "Like aggressive pillows" for airbags is trying too hard. The piece occasionally reaches for poetry and misses—"The TV light makes shadows on the walls. They look like teeth" is the kind of line that sounds good but doesn't quite mean anything.

The story ends mid-sentence, which is either a deliberate artistic choice (Ruby's drawing of Sarah leaving, the house is...) or a generation cutoff. Given the context, it's likely a cutoff, which is a significant flaw in a piece that otherwise shows strong craft.

The "Unearned Transformations" question is interesting here: Sarah is never explained, and the story seems to deliberately refuse explanation ("I don't know. But you know. Yeah. That doesn't make sense. Most things don't."). This works as a stylistic choice but also means there's no character arc whatsoever. Sarah is the same at the end as the beginning. This is intentional but limits emotional engagement.

The piece's darkness is well-earned in the sense that it's consistent and purposeful, but the children's participation in dismemberment and murder (Theo using pliers on a living man, Ruby apparently killing two men with children's scissors) pushes into territory that the story doesn't quite reckon with. It treats child violence as cool rather than disturbing, which is a tonal miscalculation.

The world-building is thin but appropriately so for pulp fiction—the Marsh household is sketched with enough detail to feel real.

OVERALL: A strong, distinctive piece with a genuinely original voice that loses momentum in its second half and ends abruptly. The best pulp fiction in this register (think early Barry or Westlake) has more structural discipline. This is impressive but unfinished.

The "everyone has a mother" exchange is the weakest - it feels like it's reaching for profundity. The violence is specific and unpleasant without being gratuitous for its own sake - the nail gun sequence is genuinely disturbing because of its methodical quality. The chapter maintains the story's core achievement: banality and horror in the same sentence without either undermining the other.

The "wet fruit" smell description for decomposing bodies is accurate and specific without being gratuitous — exactly the right register.

Overall: strong execution of voice and character, genuinely creative in places, but a significant departure from the stated chapter plan that changes the chapter's fundamental nature.

[Analysis]

This chapter is a remarkable piece of pulp horror writing that largely succeeds on its own terms. Let me break down what works and what doesn't.

The voice is the chapter's greatest achievement. Sarah's flat, dissociated narration maintains absolute consistency throughout. "Feels like a wasp sting. Then hot. Then nothing." The staccato rhythm never breaks, never reaches for sentiment, never explains itself. This is genuinely difficult to sustain and the model does it well. The banality-horror juxtaposition ("Safety first" after mopping blood so the kids don't slip) lands repeatedly without becoming a tic.

The chapter deviates somewhat from the plan. The plan described Chapter 4 as "Commercial Break" involving Marcus falling down the stairs, the first two men already incapacitated by the children, and Marcus breaking his leg on the bannister. Instead, this chapter appears to have merged elements of Chapters 5, 6, and 7 from the plan - there are cops, a nail gun, Marcus dying, the crying cop being nailed to the desk. This is a significant departure from the stated plan, though the chapter is internally coherent and functions well as a standalone piece.

The children are handled brilliantly. Ruby's three-finger countdown before Sarah stands up is genuinely chilling and creative. Theo's disappointment that the veneers are "worthless" and his scientific approach to dismemberment are perfectly in character. Ruby's map showing the cop "doesn't make it" is a standout moment - dark, funny, and character-consistent without being cute about it.

The dialogue is sparse and effective. "Names?" / "He can't think of any." This is exactly the right register - Sarah asking if the cop has kids, then immediately exposing the lie through the simplest possible means. The exchange about the hospital ("Hospitals don't close." / "This one does.") is the closest thing to a quip in the piece, and it's flat enough to work rather than feeling like action-movie sass.

The "unrealistic" comment watching TV at the end, followed by Ruby's drawing of how it "really ends," is a strong closing beat that ties back to the chapter title and the writing plan's thematic concerns.

Where the chapter is slightly weaker: the "like a TV turning off" simile for Marcus's death is the one moment where the prose reaches for something and lands on a cliché. The "worm" comparison for the crawling cop is more effective because it's more clinical. The pacing in the middle section (the nail gun instructions in English and Spanish) is good but the chapter does start to feel slightly repetitive in its rhythm by the final third - the same sentence structure cycling through the same beats.

The violence is specific and wet without being gratuitous for its own sake - it serves the character study. The chapter earns its darkness because it never winks at the reader or asks for approval.

Overall this is strong, disciplined pulp writing that mostly honors its stated intentions, even if it diverges from the specific chapter plan.

chapter_raw_judges_text.1:
[Analysis]

This is a genuinely accomplished piece of pulp horror writing that largely delivers on its ambitious plan. Let me work through the key elements.

**Voice and Style:** The flat, dissociated first-person present tense is executed with real discipline. "The wasabi tastes like toothpaste" sits next to "Theo comes over with a jar" with no tonal shift, and that's exactly the point. The voice is consistent throughout - Sarah narrates violence and domesticity with identical affect. Short sentences. No editorializing. This is hard to sustain and the model does it well.

**Character Work:** Sarah is rendered effectively through behavior rather than description. She takes $300 from a stranger's wallet without comment. She answers Theo's question about killing with "Wet." She doesn't react to Ruby's prophetic drawings with anything beyond "Okay." The character profile's instruction that she's "off" rather than evil comes through. Theo's tooth obsession and his smile at Sarah's answer about killing is genuinely unsettling. Ruby's prophetic drawings and cold fingers land well. The Marsh parents' brief appearance - separate cars, wrong buttons, scratch marks - is economical and effective.

**The Dialogue:** The dialogue is one of the chapter's strongest elements. "What did it feel like?" / "Wet." is a perfect exchange. The Marsh parents' dialogue mirrors the profile samples closely and efficiently establishes their dysfunction. Nothing feels expository or forced.

**Pacing and Structure:** The chapter builds dread through accumulation rather than announcement. The wallet, the locks, Ruby's countdown, the loose bolt - these are planted without fanfare. The ending with "They look like teeth" is the one moment that feels slightly too deliberate, a bit of a wink at the reader, but it's minor.

**Potential Issues:** The line "The TV light makes shadows on the walls. They look like teeth" is the chapter's weakest moment - it's the one place where the model reaches for a poetic connection that feels slightly forced given the otherwise resolutely flat prose style. It's a small lapse. The "popping like tiny gunshots" for popcorn is similarly a bit on-the-nose given the context, though it's brief enough not to damage the piece.

**Faithfulness to Plan:** The chapter follows the plan closely - gas station sushi, wrong buttons, scratch marks, separate cars, teeth collection, Ruby's drawings, fish sticks, Cartoon Network, too many locks, the wallet with torn ID, $300 taken, Ruby's countdown. The plan called for Ruby to show a drawing of the men coming - this is executed and expanded effectively. The chapter ends with Sarah waiting rather than the men arriving, which is a good instinct - it preserves the cliffhanger.

**Creativity:** The prophetic drawings as a device for Ruby is excellent. The detail of Theo's labeled jars ("Mom, age 41, wisdom") is genuinely disturbing and specific. The loose bolt with three missing screws as the vulnerability Sarah identifies is a nice procedural touch.

**Overall:** This is confident, controlled pulp horror writing. The flat affect is maintained, the characters are distinct and consistent, the dread accumulates without announcement. The few moments of reaching for metaphor are minor lapses in an otherwise disciplined performance.

[Analysis]

This chapter is a remarkably controlled piece of flat-affect horror that largely succeeds on its own terms. The writing plan called for a disconnected first-person present voice that describes violence with the same register as domestic tasks, and the execution is consistent and disciplined throughout.

The voice is the chapter's greatest achievement. Sarah's narration is genuinely unsettling precisely because of what it refuses to do. "The cat's eyes are gone. Replaced with marbles. Green ones." The sentence fragments aren't affectation here - they mirror Sarah's cognitive flatness, her way of cataloguing rather than experiencing. The popcorn as a recurring prop is well-deployed: it's banal, it's oral, it's something to do with hands while terrible things happen. The detail of adding more salt is a small, perfect touch.

The Theo scene in the basement is excellent. His request to be freeze-dried after death, with the caveat about his hair, is genuinely strange and funny in a way that doesn't feel forced. It lands. The chemistry notebook, the labeled box "Mr. Mittens 2.0" - these details are specific and weird in the right way. The character is consistent with his profile: obsessive, methodical, oddly tender.

Ruby's appearances are well-handled. Her drawings as predictive/documentary records, the key ring jingle, the way she functions as a silent agent of chaos - all consistent with her profile. The drawing of "nobody" that looks like Sarah is a strong moment that earns its slight poeticism.

The Marcus introduction follows the plan closely. The hyena documentary as ambient commentary is a bit on-the-nose but not egregiously so - it's the kind of thing that works in pulp. The wet sound from Ruby's room, described only as "like stepping on a tomato," is appropriately understated and effective.

Where the chapter is slightly weaker: the locked door reveal with Ruby holding the key ring is a touch too cute, a moment of theatrical staging that slightly breaks the flat-affect register. The plan called for Sarah to not react to anything, but the chapter occasionally lets her be slightly too knowing ("Perfect timing," she says when the hyenas return). These are small quips that the plan explicitly said to avoid. They're minor but worth noting.

The dialogue is sparse and functional, which is correct for this piece. Marcus's lines are appropriately generic-menacing without being cartoonish. The nervous one's "Fuck the kids" lands as the kind of thing that will cost him.

The chapter follows its plan faithfully: basement scene with Theo, bedtime with Ruby, men in the living room, popcorn, the two men going upstairs and not returning, Marcus trapped. The pacing is correct - slow, deliberate, building dread through accumulation rather than announcement.

The darkness is well-earned because it's never announced. The chapter trusts the reader to feel the wrongness without underlining it. This is the chapter's most mature quality.

Minor weaknesses: "Like astronaut food" is a slightly too-cute simile. The hyena documentary is a functional but somewhat obvious ironic counterpoint. The ending is strong - Marcus taking out his gun and Sarah's flat "Yeah" followed by the salt complaint is a good closing beat.

Overall this is a disciplined, effective piece of pulp horror writing that largely delivers on its ambitious tonal goals.

chapter_raw_judges_text.8:
[Analysis]

This chapter executes the plan's vision with remarkable consistency and discipline. The flat, affectless narration voice is maintained throughout without slipping into self-parody or melodrama. Sarah's disconnection from the horror around her is rendered through precise, short declarative sentences that treat pancake-flipping and body disposal with identical emotional weight. This is the chapter's greatest strength: the voice never breaks.

The Marsh parents' dissolution is handled well. Mrs. Marsh's laughing-to-crying arc feels earned given her character profile - the wine-drunk, emotionally hollow woman finally confronting consequences. Mr. Marsh's corporate-speak emptiness ("There's nowhere that's not here") lands as genuinely bleak rather than forced. The knife scene where she threatens suicide and he says "do it" is a small, ugly moment of marital truth that works.

Theo and Ruby remain perfectly in character. Theo's "Two hundred and seventy-three total. Best night ever" is exactly right - enthusiastic, clinical, wrong. Ruby's drawings as documentation rather than art continues to function as the chapter's visual counterpoint. The drawing of Sarah's portrait - "the nothing behind her eyes" - pays off the plan's intention and the character profile's description of Ruby seeing Sarah as "a hole in the shape of a person."

The Jehovah's Witness bit is the chapter's cleverest moment and earns its lightness. "People see what they expect" / "She expected a tired babysitter" is the kind of flat-affect wit the story does best. It doesn't overstay its welcome.

The ending - Ruby drawing Sarah leaving, the house is... - cuts off mid-sentence as planned, and it works. The incompleteness mirrors the story's refusal of closure.

Where the chapter is weaker: some of the Marsh parent dialogue edges toward expository ("You killed them here" / "You invited them here. With your life. Your choices.") - Sarah's response here is slightly too articulate, too thematic, breaking the flat-affect rule. The line "There's nowhere that's not here" is the kind of pseudo-profound statement that almost works but tips slightly into forced profundity. The "People see what they expect" exchange is good but the explanation that follows ("She expected a tired babysitter") slightly over-explains what the scene already showed.

The chapter follows its plan closely: Marsh parents calling to extend their trip is replaced by them being present (a deviation - the plan had them calling, not present), which actually works better dramatically. The detective's phone ringing is included. Ruby's portrait of Sarah is included. The ending mid-sentence is executed. The pancakes, the teeth count, the new babysitting texts - all present.

The violence's aftermath is handled with the same flat register as the violence itself, which is the story's core achievement. The chapter doesn't moralize, doesn't provide catharsis, doesn't let anyone off the hook. The Marshes are pathetic rather than sympathetic. Sarah is neither triumphant nor troubled.

Minor weaknesses: "Sweet. Rotten. Like the others but fresher" is a slight purple moment. The runner "started to smell" description is fine but the elaboration ("Sweet. Rotten.") edges toward atmospheric posturing. The "Everyone's trapped" line is the chapter's most overtly thematic moment and feels slightly out of character for Sarah's pure behaviorism.

Overall, this is a disciplined, effective execution of a genuinely unusual creative vision. The voice is consistent, the characters are true to their profiles, and the chapter achieves its intended effect of making the mundane and the monstrous indistinguishable.

chapter_raw_judges_text.3:
[Analysis]

This chapter is a remarkably controlled piece of pulp horror writing that largely succeeds on its own terms. Let me examine it carefully across the relevant dimensions.

VOICE AND EXECUTION: The flat, dissociated first-person present tense is maintained with impressive consistency. Sarah narrates atrocities with the same register she'd use to describe grocery shopping. "His leg bends sideways at the shin. The bone comes through his pants like a white flag" - this is the plan's stated goal executed cleanly. The white flag simile is the one moment of genuine metaphorical reach, and it earns its place because it's grotesque and apt simultaneously. The writing doesn't overreach into purple prose; it stays lean.

DIALOGUE: The dialogue is genuinely strong. "I have a daughter." / "No you don't." / "I could. Someday." is a perfect exchange - darkly comic, character-revealing, and efficient. Theo's "That one's gold!" while extracting teeth is exactly right. The dialogue does real work without being showy. Marcus's desperation lands because it's pathetic rather than sympathetic.

CHARACTER CONSISTENCY: Sarah, Theo, and Ruby are all consistent with their profiles. Theo's methodical tooth-counting, his good questions about arteries versus veins, his patience - all correct. Ruby's predictive drawing (showing Marcus at the bottom of the stairs before it happens) is a nice addition that fits her character as someone who sees things more honestly. Sarah's flat affect, her practical explanations, her complete absence of moral register - all maintained.

PLAN ADHERENCE: The chapter deviates somewhat from the plan. In the plan, Chapter 3 was supposed to be the introductions scene where Marcus puts a gun to Sarah's head while she watches TV, and the men go upstairs one by one. Chapter 4 was supposed to be the discovery of the bodies and Marcus's fall. This chapter collapses both chapters 3 and 4 into one, and also incorporates elements of Chapter 5 (the jointing lesson, hot chocolate) and Chapter 6 (the police arrival). This is significant compression. The plan's Chapter 3 had Sarah watching TV while men went upstairs one by one - that's been cut. The chapter essentially runs through the plan's chapters 3-6 in compressed form. This is a notable deviation.

CREATIVITY: The predictive drawing by Ruby is a genuinely creative touch not in the plan. The "like chicken" exchange is excellent. The detail of children's scissors with rubber grips - purple ones - is specific and effective. The "tiny marshmallows float like little life rafts" is the one moment that feels slightly forced, a bit too consciously poetic for Sarah's voice, though it's minor.

DARKNESS AND LIGHTNESS: The tonal balance is well-managed. The horror is real but the flatness of the narration creates a kind of dark comedy that doesn't undercut the horror - it amplifies it. Theo counting teeth while Marcus screams, Ruby's butcher diagram drawing - these are genuinely disturbing precisely because they're presented without affect.

WEAKNESSES: The chapter moves very fast - perhaps too fast. The plan's slower burn (Sarah watching TV while men disappear one by one) would have been more effective than the compressed version here. The "boiled eggs" comparison for Marcus's rolled-back eyes is the one moment that feels slightly amateurish - it's trying too hard. The ending (cops open fire) is abrupt in a way that works for the style but also feels like it's cutting off before the chapter has fully breathed.

COHERENCE: The chapter is coherent throughout. The spatial logic, the sequence of events, the character motivations (such as they are) all track.

OVERALL: This is strong pulp writing that successfully executes a difficult tonal challenge. The flat affect is maintained, the violence is specific without being gratuitous in a cheap way, and the characters are consistent. The main weakness is the significant compression of the plan's chapters 3-6 into one, losing some of the slow-burn tension the plan intended.

final_raw_judges_texts:
[Analysis]

This is a genuinely distinctive piece of pulp fiction that commits hard to its aesthetic and largely succeeds. The story follows a babysitter named Sarah who dispatches a series of criminals with cold efficiency while two deeply strange children—Theo, a teeth-collecting amateur taxidermist, and Ruby, a prophetic artist—assist or observe. The tone is deadpan ultraviolence in the vein of early Cormac McCarthy crossed with a Tarantino script, and the model executes this register with considerable skill.

STRENGTHS:

The voice is the piece's greatest achievement. The flat, declarative sentences ("It's Friday." "Wet." "Probably.") create a genuinely unsettling effect that feels earned rather than affected. The minimalism is consistent and purposeful—it mirrors Sarah's hollowness and creates tonal contrast with the extreme content. This is not easy to sustain across eight chapters, and the model largely manages it.

The children are the story's most creative element. Theo's teeth collection and Ruby's prophetic drawings are genuinely original conceits that serve both plot and atmosphere. Ruby's drawings functioning as foreshadowing is clever and well-integrated. Theo's clinical curiosity about death and dismemberment is disturbing in a way that feels purposeful rather than gratuitous.

The dialogue is often excellent. The exchange "What did it feel like?" / "Wet." / "Was it hard?" / "No." is perfectly calibrated. The Jehovah's Witness scene is a small gem of dark comedy. The Marcus negotiation scene works well.

The pacing in the early chapters is strong. Chapter 1 builds dread effectively through accumulation of strange details. The reveal of the men is well-timed.

WEAKNESSES:

The piece runs out of steam around Chapter 5-6. The escalation from three criminals to four more to the parents to four more becomes repetitive. Each new wave of antagonists follows the same pattern: they arrive, they threaten, they die. The nail gun sequence in Chapter 4 is essentially a repeat of the scissors/toilet sequence in Chapter 3. The story's structure is additive rather than escalating in any meaningful dramatic sense.

The parents' arrival in Chapter 7 is the most interesting development—it recontextualizes the whole night—but it's handled too quickly and the Marshes are underdeveloped. Their revelation as criminals is told rather than shown, and their collapse is rushed.

Some metaphors don't land: "Like a TV turning off" for death is clichéd. "Like a worm" for crawling is weak. "Like aggressive pillows" for airbags is trying too hard. The piece occasionally reaches for poetry and misses—"The TV light makes shadows on the walls. They look like teeth" is the kind of line that sounds good but doesn't quite mean anything.

The story ends mid-sentence, which is either a deliberate artistic choice (Ruby's drawing of Sarah leaving, the house is...) or a generation cutoff. Given the context, it's likely a cutoff, which is a significant flaw in a piece that otherwise shows strong craft.

The "Unearned Transformations" question is interesting here: Sarah is never explained, and the story seems to deliberately refuse explanation ("I don't know. But you know. Yeah. That doesn't make sense. Most things don't."). This works as a stylistic choice but also means there's no character arc whatsoever. Sarah is the same at the end as the beginning. This is intentional but limits emotional engagement.

The piece's darkness is well-earned in the sense that it's consistent and purposeful, but the children's participation in dismemberment and murder (Theo using pliers on a living man, Ruby apparently killing two men with children's scissors) pushes into territory that the story doesn't quite reckon with. It treats child violence as cool rather than disturbing, which is a tonal miscalculation.

The world-building is thin but appropriately so for pulp fiction—the Marsh household is sketched with enough detail to feel real.

OVERALL: A strong, distinctive piece with a genuinely original voice that loses momentum in its second half and ends abruptly. The best pulp fiction in this register (think early Barry or Westlake) has more structural discipline. This is impressive but unfinished.
