# Rhys Morgan

**Background & Personality:** Rhys left Merthyr Tydfil at 21 for drama school in London, changed his surname from Pritchard because his agent said Morgan was "more marketable." Has been in three Fast & Furious knockoffs and a superhero film where he played "British Villain #2." His mum still lives in the same council house; he bought her a new build in Cardiff but she refused to move. Sends money home but hasn't visited in three years. Desperately insecure about his intelligence—failed his A-levels, reads Wikipedia summaries of classic books before parties. Has a genuine love for terrible action films, not ironically, actually thinks Commando is high art. Laughs too loud at other people's jokes. Touches people when he talks—arms, shoulders, unconscious need for connection. Still quotes his dad who died when he was fifteen, always starts with "My old man used to say..." 

**Physicality:** 5'11" but tells people he's 6 foot. Gym-built in that specifically Hollywood way—too defined to be natural. Dyes his hair darker than its natural brown. Has a scar through his left eyebrow from a school fight he tells different stories about. Walks like he's always being filmed—shoulders back, slight swagger that disappears when he's actually relaxed. Rubs his jaw when lying. Expensive veneers that make his smile too white for his face. Wears designer clothes that never quite fit right—he's between sizes and too vain to size up. Has reading glasses he never wears in public.

**Motives & Relationships:** Wants to be taken seriously as an actor but also terrified of actual failure, so keeps taking safe roles. Sends his ex (actress Madeleine Cross) long texts at 2am he deletes in the morning. His manager, Simon, is the closest thing he has to a friend but he's not sure Simon actually likes him. Has a therapist in LA he lies to. Wants his mum to be proud but she keeps asking when he'll be in something she's heard of. Three-year relationship with Madeleine ended because she said being with him was "like dating a golden retriever who could do push-ups." Still hurt about it.

**Voice Samples:**
"Right, yeah, absolutely. That's brilliant. Really brilliant. Is it Wordsworth? No, wait—Keats? Fuck, sorry, I know this one..."

"My old man used to say you can tell everything about a person by their shoes. Course, he wore the same pair for fifteen years, so not sure what that said about him. These? They're... Italian. Cost more than... sorry, that's wanky, isn't it? Christ, I'm shit at this."

Internal: *She's looking at me. Say something clever. Shakespeare. No, she'll know you googled it. Just smile. Not that much, you look mental. Christ, why is this so hard? It's just talking. You talk for a living. Technically.*

# Cerys Davies

**Background & Personality:** Inherited the shop when her gran died two years ago. Was doing a PhD in Contemporary Literature at Cardiff, quit to run the shop out of guilt and some romantic notion about "keeping it in the family." Regrets it every month when she does the accounts. Has an English degree she's defensive about using. Watches Love Island religiously but tells people she doesn't own a TV. Orders the same Chinese takeaway every Friday (sweet and sour chicken, fried rice, no prawn crackers because of a childhood incident). Has been engaged twice, never married, both times said yes when she meant "maybe" and let it drag out until they left. Bites her nails, paints them to stop, bites them anyway. Secretly writes book reviews on Goodreads under a fake name, surprisingly harsh.

**Physicality:** 5'6", carries herself like she's trying to take up less space. Dark hair she cuts herself when anxious—currently uneven. Wears the same rotation of five jumpers, all slightly pilled. Has her gran's hands—long fingers, always cold. Touches her neck when nervous. Smile comes slowly but changes her whole face when it's real. Terrible poker face—every thought shows. Needs glasses but wears contacts, eyes always slightly red. Has a tattoo of a Virginia Woolf quote on her ribcage she got at uni, regrets it because it's slightly misquoted. Stands with her weight on one hip, arms crossed, defensive by default.

**Motives & Relationships:** Wants to want the shop, if that makes sense. Feels like she should be grateful but mostly feels trapped. Sister Sian is married with kids, proper adult life, makes Cerys feel perpetually behind. Mum drops in daily with gossip and unsolicited advice about her love life. Dad thinks she should sell to developers, won't say it directly. Still in love with her ex Owen from uni who's now married, teaches at Cardiff, comes in sometimes to buy poetry. Tells herself she's waiting for something but doesn't know what.

**Voice Samples:**
"No, that's not... you're thinking of Byron. Everyone thinks that's Byron but it's actually... sorry, doesn't matter. It's £8.99. Do you want a bag?"

"I'm not impressed by the fame thing, if that's what you're doing. I've seen your films. Well, some of them. One and a half. The one with the helicopter was... loud."

Internal: *Oh God, he's doing the smile. The Hollywood smile. Does he practice that? Probably. Definitely. Why is it working? It's not working. It's a bit working. Stop staring at his arms. Christ, Cerys, you're thirty-four, not fourteen.*

# Sian Davies (Cerys's sister)

**Background & Personality:** Younger by two years but has her life "together"—husband Marc, two kids, teaches primary school. Secretly thrilled by Cerys's drama. Pretends to be above celebrity gossip but has Google alerts set for Rhys Morgan now. Drinks rosé at 4pm "because it's practically juice." Gives advice through questions: "Have you thought about maybe not being so difficult?" Loves her sister but also enjoys being the stable one for once.

**Physicality:** Cerys but softer—same features but filled out from kids and contentment. Wears Boden, owns it. Always slightly harried, hair in a mum bun that actually looks good.

**Voice Sample:**
"I'm not saying you should shag him, but I'm also not saying you shouldn't. God, when did you get so precious about things? He's fit and he's clearly gagging for it. What's complicated about that?"

# Mrs Pritchard (Regular customer)

**Background & Personality:** 73, husband recently died, comes in daily for company not books. Buys one paperback a week, same genre (crime), same complaint ("not as good as Agatha Christie"). Knows everyone's business, shares it freely. Has no idea who Rhys is, doesn't care. Thinks Cerys needs to "get on with it" regarding everything—life, love, the shop.

**Physicality:** Tiny, imperious, same coat regardless of weather. Smells of Pears soap and cigarettes she claims she doesn't smoke.

**Voice Sample:**
"You've got a customer, love. Tall one. Looks lost. Bit pleased with himself if you ask me. Is that new? The thing with your hair? Well. It's certainly different."
step_outputs.6:
# Chapter 1: Emergency Exit

The door slammed open hard enough to rattle the Victorian crime novels.

"Shit, sorry, I—" The man stopped mid-apology, breathing hard, designer trainers squeaking on the worn floor. "Can I just—there's photographers—"

Cerys looked up from her cheese sandwich, a string of pickle hanging from her mouth. She sucked it in. "We're closed for lunch."

"Your door was open."

"For air. Not customers."

He was already moving deeper into the shop, crushing himself between Biography and Local Interest. A camera flash went off outside. Then another.

"Please." His voice cracked slightly. "Five minutes. I'll buy something."

She recognized him then—not immediately, but in pieces. The jaw from that terrible submarine film. The eyes from the posters. Rhys something. Morgan? The Welsh one who wasn't actually Welsh anymore.

"You're dripping on my books," she said.

He looked down. He was. Rain from his designer jacket making small puddles on a stack of marked-down hardbacks. "Christ, sorry, I'll—"

He shrugged off the jacket, knocked over the entire display of Welsh poetry. Twenty-three books cascaded across the floor. The shop cat, Mr. Darcy, yowled and shot upstairs.

"Fuck." He dropped to his knees, grabbing books. "Sorry, God, I'm not usually—'I will arise and go now, and go to Innisfree.' Yeats, right? I know poetry."

"That's Wordsworth." It wasn't. It was Yeats. But his desperate need to impress annoyed her.

"Right. Yes. Wordsworth." He was still kneeling, holding a collection of R.S. Thomas like it might explode. "I knew that."

Another flash outside. He flinched, duck-walked behind the counter where she was sitting. Too close. She could smell his cologne—expensive, trying too hard.

"You can't be back here."

"Sorry, yes, I'll just—" He stood too fast, banged his head on the till. "Shit."

"There's a stockroom." She pointed. "Through there. Don't touch anything."

He disappeared. She heard a crash. Then: "Found it!"

Cerys returned to her sandwich. Through the window, she counted three photographers, maybe four. They were patient, she'd give them that. Smoking, checking phones, waiting.

"Diolch yn fawr." His head appeared around the doorframe. "That's thank you. In Welsh."

"I know what it means."

"Right. Course. You're Welsh."

"So are you, supposedly."

"I am!" He emerged fully, indignant. "Merthyr born and bred. Fy enw i yw Rhys."

His pronunciation was painful. Like someone reading phonetically from a guidebook.

"Dwi ddim yn credu bod gen ti Gymraeg," she said flatly.

His face went blank. "Yeah, absolutely. Same to you."

She nearly smiled. Caught herself.

Mrs. Pritchard chose that moment to shuffle in, Tuesday crime novel clutched in her liver-spotted hand.

"You've not got the new Rankin, have you, love? This one was rubbish. Too much swearing." She squinted at Rhys. "Who's this then? Bit tall for you."

"He's not—he's a customer."

"Am I?" Rhys looked pleased. "Yes. I am. I need... books."

"They're all around you," Cerys said.

"Right. Yes." He grabbed the nearest one. "Perfect. This one."

"That's volume three of a history of Welsh mining."

"I love mining. History of it. Especially Welsh."

Mrs. Pritchard was reaching for something on a high shelf, straining on her tiptoes. "Give us a hand, would you?"

Rhys practically vaulted the counter in his eagerness to help. Grabbed the shelf for leverage. It creaked ominously.

"Just the book, not the whole bloody thing," Mrs. Pritchard said.

He extracted the book carefully, handed it over like an offering. She patted his arm. "Good lad. Bit eager, aren't you?"

Outside, the photographers were multiplying. Six now. Maybe seven.

"I should go," Rhys said, but didn't move.

"They'll follow you," Cerys pointed out.

"Yeah."

"Must be nice. Being so important."

"It's not—I'm not—" He ran his hand through his hair. It stood up at strange angles. "It's just because of the thing with Madeleine."

"I don't know who that is."

"My ex. She was in that Netflix thing. With the horses."

"I don't have Netflix."

He stared at her. "Everyone has Netflix."

"I have books."

Mrs. Pritchard snorted. "She's got Netflix. Saw her watching that thing with the murders on her phone last week."

Cerys felt her face heat. "That was research."

"Course it was, love." Mrs. Pritchard shuffled toward the door. "Ta-ra then. Try not to break anything else, tall boy."

The bell jangled. The photographers perked up, then deflated when they saw her.

Rhys was rolling up his sleeves, apparently settling in. His forearms were ridiculous. Like someone had designed them specifically to make sleeves look restrictive.

Cerys realized she was staring. He noticed her noticing. They both looked away.

"So," he said. "The mining book."

"You don't want the mining book."

"I might."

"It's £45."

"Bargain."

"And the poetry you destroyed?"

He looked at the scattered books. "All of them?"

"You break it, you buy it."

He calculated. She could actually see him doing maths. "How much?"

"£73. Including the mining."

He pulled out his wallet. Black. Expensive. Slightly too shiny. "Do you take cards?"

"Machine's broken."

It wasn't.

He counted out cash. New twenties that looked like play money. "Keep the change."

"That's a £7 tip."

"For the cat. Trauma counseling."

This time she did smile, just slightly. He caught it, looked absurdly pleased with himself.

The photographers were getting restless. One knocked on the window. Rhys gave them a very specific hand gesture.

"Charming," Cerys said.

"Sorry. They bring out the worst in me."

"This isn't the worst?"

"This is me trying very hard to be likeable."

"How's that working out?"

He looked at her, then at the destroyed poetry section, the mining book he didn't want, the photographers outside. "Honestly? I've had better days."

The admission was unexpected. Real. For a moment, neither of them said anything.

Then his phone rang. The Superman theme. He winced. "I should—it's my manager."

She waved him toward the stockroom. He disappeared again. She heard his voice change—smoother, more American somehow. "Simon! Yeah, small hiccup..."

Cerys picked up the scattered poetry. One had his wet handprint on the cover. She should have been annoyed. Instead, she found herself checking if his number might be there somewhere.

It wasn't.

But he'd left his jacket behind.

Definitely on purpose.
step_outputs.7:
# Chapter 2: The Apology Tour

Wednesday. 10:47 am. He was back.

"Bore da!" Rhys announced, shouldering through the door with a coffee tray. "That's good morning. I looked it up."

"It's nearly eleven," Cerys said, not looking up from her accounts. The numbers were depressing. The numbers were always depressing.

"Still morning though." He set a cup in front of her. "Oat milk latte. Extra shot."

She stared at it. "I drink tea."

"Tea?"

"Builder's. Three sugars."

"Who drinks three sugars?"

"People who like sugar."

He deflated slightly, then rallied. "Prynhawn da, then. Good afternoon. For later."

His pronunciation made it sound like he was choking. She winced. "Please don't."

"I'm trying to reconnect with my roots."

"Your roots are in Merthyr. They speak English in Merthyr."

"They speak Welsh too."

"Do they though?"

He picked up the untouched latte, took a sip himself. Made a face. "Christ, that's awful. How do people drink oat milk?"

"Vegans, mostly."

"Are you vegan?"

"I was eating a cheese sandwich yesterday."

"Right. Yes." He wandered over to her desk, peered at the spreadsheet. "That's a lot of red."

She slammed the laptop shut. "Did you just come for your jacket?"

"My jacket?"

"The one you deliberately left."

"I didn't—" He stopped. Grinned. "Fine. But also to apologize. Properly. For yesterday."

"You already apologized. With money."

"That was transactional. This is heartfelt." He pressed his hand to his chest dramatically, then dropped it. "Sorry, that was wanky. I'm nervous."

"Why?"

"You make me nervous."

"I'm literally nobody."

"You're not—that's not—" He picked up a book from her desk. Put it down. Picked it up again. "Can I help? With something? Anything? I could sign books. For an event or something."

"Why would anyone want you to sign books you didn't write?"

"People are weird? I signed a woman's arm once. At a Tesco."

"Was it your autobiography?"

"I haven't written one."

"Shocking."

He laughed. "You're mean. I like it. Everyone's usually so—'Oh Rhys, you're wonderful, tell us about the helicopter scene again.'"

"The helicopter scene was ridiculous."

His face lit up. "You saw it?"

"Ten minutes on someone's phone. Over their shoulder. On a train."

"That still counts." He was absurdly pleased. "What else have you seen?"

"The one where you're a rogue detective."

"Death Warrant or Final Justice?"

"There's two?"

"Three actually. Final Justice 2: No Appeal comes out at Christmas."

"Catchy."

"It's shit. They're all shit." He said it cheerfully, like discussing weather. "But the helicopter one—Skyfire—that was based on a book."

"No it wasn't."

"It was! Michael Crichton. Well, 'inspired by.'"

"That's not the same as based on."

"The main character reads. In one scene. He's holding a book."

"A book you clearly never opened, since you spent the whole film shirtless shooting people."

"You watched more than ten minutes."

She'd watched all of it. Twice. Once drunk with Sian. "It was ambient noise while I did inventory."

"Inventory must have taken two hours and eighteen minutes then."

"There's a lot of books here."

He was grinning now, moving closer to her desk. That cologne again. Why did he have to smell so unnecessarily good?

"I could help," he said. "With inventory."

"Can you alphabetize?"

"I know the alphabet."

"All of it?"

"Most of it. The important bits."

A delivery van pulled up outside. The driver started unloading boxes. Heavy ones.

"Shit," Cerys muttered. "My back's still fucked from yesterday."

Rhys was already moving. "I've got it."

"You don't—"

But he was out the door, chatting to the driver, lifting boxes like they weighed nothing. His t-shirt rode up when he reached for the highest one. She absolutely did not notice the strip of skin above his jeans.

"Where do these go?" He was holding three boxes at once. Showing off.

"Stockroom. Carefully. They're books, not weights."

He disappeared into the back. No crash this time. He was learning.

They developed a rhythm—him bringing boxes, her checking invoice numbers, him talking constantly about nothing. His last film shoot in Bulgaria ("Everyone got food poisoning except me because I only ate McDonald's"). His mother ("She asks when I'll be in something clever, like Poirot"). His trainer in LA ("Nazi with a six-pack").

"You don't have to narrate everything," she said, but not unkindly.

"Sorry. I talk when I'm nervous."

"You're still nervous?"

"Bit, yeah."

"Why?"

He set down the last box. Rubbed his jaw. "You don't like me."

"I don't know you."

"But you don't like what you know."

"I didn't say that."

"You didn't have to. You've got one of those faces. Every thought just..." He gestured at her face. "Right there."

She touched her cheek self-consciously. "I do not."

"You're thinking right now that I'm an arrogant prick who can't act."

She wasn't. She was thinking about his forearms again. "I'm thinking you talk too much."

"Same thing really."

"It's not."

They stood there, him sweating slightly from the boxes, her clutching her invoice list. The shop felt smaller with him in it.

"I should go," he said but didn't move.

"Your jacket's behind the counter."

"Right. My jacket." He retrieved it, found a pen in the pocket, wrote something on a bookmark. "My number. In case you need... more boxes moved. Or alphabetizing. Or someone to watch Skyfire with and point out all the plot holes."

"That would take longer than the film."

"See? You did watch it properly."

She took the bookmark. His handwriting was terrible. "This could be anyone's number."

"07799..."

"I can read it."

"Can you though? That might be a three. Or an eight."

"It's a three."

"Is it?"

She looked closer. It definitely wasn't a three.

"Fine, it's an eight."

He backed toward the door, knocked into a display. Caught it. "I'm usually much cooler than this."

"I don't believe you."

"Fair." He paused at the door. "The coffee's still here."

"I don't drink coffee."

"I know. But maybe..." He shrugged. "Tomorrow you might."

After he left, she sat at her desk, staring at the numbers that still didn't add up. Picked up the latte. Cold now. Took a sip.

Still awful.

But she drank it anyway, and hated herself for finding his desperate need for her approval endearing. She was thirty-four, for Christ's sake. Not fifteen.

Her phone buzzed. Sian: "Mum says Rhys Morgan was in your shop AGAIN!!!! DETAILS NOW."

Cerys turned it face down. Picked up the bookmark with his terrible handwriting.

Definitely an eight.
step_outputs.8:
# Chapter 3: Thursday Night Mistake

Thursday. 11:13 pm.

Cerys was lying on the shop floor, surrounded by empty wine bottles and the remnants of book club. Three members this month—her, Sian, and Bethan from the post office who only came for gossip.

"I'm just saying," Bethan slurred, horizontal on the Poetry section carpet. "His arms in that helicopter scene."

"We're supposed to be discussing Hamnet," Cerys said.

"Fuck Hamnet. Dead kid. Sad. Done. Now back to Rhys Morgan being in your shop."

"Twice," Sian added, unhelpfully. "Mum counted."

Cerys's phone sat on her stomach, rising and falling with her breathing. That bookmark with his terrible number had migrated to her pocket. Then her purse. Now her phone case.

"We watched Skyfire," Bethan announced. "Instead of reading."

"I noticed," Cerys said.

"You defended it," Sian said. "You literally said the plot made sense if you didn't think about it."

"I was drunk."

"You're drunk now."

This was true. The room had a pleasant spin to it. Mr. Darcy was sitting on her chest, purring aggressively.

"Text him," Bethan said.

"No."

"Just text him," Sian said. "Something casual."

"It's eleven at night."

"So?"

"Nothing casual happens at eleven at night."

Her phone buzzed. Instagram notification. She opened it accidentally-on-purpose. Rhys had posted a story—hotel bar, Cardiff by the look of it. Glass of something amber. Caption: "Method acting for tomorrow (I play an alcoholic detective)."

It was deleted ten seconds later.

"He's in Cardiff," she heard herself say.

"TEXT HIM," both women shouted.

She typed: "Your handwriting is shit."

Deleted it.

Typed: "Watching Skyfire for book club."

Sent it.

"Oh my God," Sian squealed. "You did it."

Three dots appeared immediately. Disappeared. Appeared again.

"Please tell me you're joking."

She typed back: "Bethan says your arms deserve an Oscar."

"Bethan has excellent taste. The rest of me was robbed."

"Your acting definitely was."

"Harsh. True. But harsh. How pissed are you?"

"Medium."

"Scale of one to ten?"

"Thursday."

"That's not a number."

"Seven. Maybe eight. We drank all the wine."

"Book club sounds fun."

"We didn't read the book."

"What was it?"

"Hamnet."

"Shakespeare's kid?"

"You know about Shakespeare's kid?"

"I'm not completely thick."

Bethan and Sian were reading over her shoulder now, breathing wine fumes onto her neck.

"Call him," Bethan whispered.

"Don't call him," Sian said.

Her phone rang.

"Shit," all three said.

Cerys answered. "Hi."

"Sorry, my finger slipped." His voice was rougher. Drinking voice.

"Your finger slipped onto my contact and then onto the call button?"

"Very slippery fingers. It's a medical condition."

"Is it?"

"No."

She could hear bar noise behind him. Music. Voices. "Where are you?"

"Cardiff. Hotel bar. Having a very sad drink alone because my co-star hates me."

"Why?"

"I might have said her dog looks like a Victorian child with consumption."

"Does it?"

"Absolutely. But apparently you're not supposed to say that."

She laughed. Bethan gave her a thumbs up. Sian mouthed 'be cool.'

"Where are you?" he asked.

"Shop floor. Doing inventory."

"At eleven at night? After seven drinks?"

"Eight. And yes. Very important inventory."

"Of what?"

She looked around. "Contemporary fiction."

"You're lying on contemporary fiction?"

"Among it."

"That sounds uncomfortable."

"Mr. Darcy's here too."

"The cat?"

"No, the actual Mr. Darcy. Very disappointing in person."

She heard him moving, the bar noise fading. A door opening. Wind.

"Are you outside?" she asked.

"Balcony. Room 412. In case you want to... I don't know. Throw rocks at me."

"From Hay? That's a very long throw."

"You could drive."

"I'm Thursday drunk, remember?"

"Right. Yes. Don't drive."

Bethan and Sian had retreated to the corner, pretending not to listen while obviously listening.

"Tell me about the worst film you've ever made," Cerys said, rolling onto her side. The cat protested, relocated.

"Oh God. Venice Vengeance."

"Never heard of it."

"Direct to DVD. I play a rogue gondolier who's secretly CIA."

"A rogue gondolier?"

"With a heart of gold and a dark past."

"Naturally."

"There's a speedboat chase through the canals."

"That seems impractical."

"The speedboat explodes."

"Of course it does."

"While I'm jumping from it. Onto another speedboat. Which also explodes."

"Why do you keep getting on exploding boats?"

"Poor judgment? It's a theme in my life."

She could hear him smoking. The little exhale.

"Disgusting habit," she said.

"I know. I'm quitting."

"When?"

"Tomorrow. Always tomorrow."

They talked. About films (he'd never seen The Princess Bride, she was horrified). About sandwiches (he thought pickle was "aggressive," she thought his preference for hummus was "aggressively middle class"). About whether cats had accents.

"Of course they do," she insisted, now in the History section. "Mr. Darcy's is very posh."

"Mr. Darcy's a Welsh cat."

"He's bilingual."

"Can he say 'Llanfairpwllgwyngyllgogerychwyrndrobwllllantysiliogogogoch'?"

"Can you?"

"No. But I'm not claiming to be bilingual."

"Your Wikipedia says you speak fluent Welsh."

"You looked at my Wikipedia?"

Fuck. "Bethan did."

"Is Bethan there?"

"No. She left. Ages ago."

This was true. Both Sian and Bethan had left around midnight, making obscene gestures as they went.

"So you're alone?"

"Yes."

"On your shop floor."

"Yes."

"Drinking."

"Not anymore. Lying down. The ceiling has a weird stain. Like Italy. If Italy had water damage."

"My ceiling has a chandelier. It's hideous."

"Poor you."

"I didn't mean—"

"I know."

Silence. But not uncomfortable. She could hear him breathing. Traffic below his balcony.

"I should let you sleep," he said.

"I'm doing inventory."

"Right. The very important drunk inventory."

"Critical to the business."

"How's that going?"

"I've counted to twelve. Several times."

He laughed. Low and tired. "What time do you open?"

"Nine."

"Christ."

"Yeah."

"I could... bring you breakfast? Hangover breakfast?"

"I don't eat breakfast."

"Everyone eats breakfast."

"I eat lunch at ten-thirty."

"That's basically breakfast."

"It's a sandwich."

"Cheese and pickle?"

He'd remembered. "Maybe."

"I'll bring you a bacon sandwich. From that place on King Street."

"I don't like bacon."

"You're lying."

She was.

"Nine?" he asked.

"I'll be here."

"Good. Yes. Right."

Neither hung up.

"This is stupid," she said.

"Very."

"We should stop talking."

"Absolutely."

They kept talking. About nothing. Everything. She migrated to Travel Writing. Then Biography. Her phone was hot against her ear.

"My battery's dying," she said.

"Mine too."

"We should—"

"Yeah."

At some point, one of them fell asleep. She woke at 3:47 am, phone dead, cheek stuck to a book cover. Charged it enough to see his last message, sent at 2:33 am:

"you snore btw"

Then: "it's quite cute"

Then: "that's weird sorry"

Then: "ignore that"

Then: "fuck"

She smiled at her phone like an idiot. Then remembered she'd have to see him in five hours. With a hangover. After snoring at him.

"Fuck," she said to Mr. Darcy.

Mr. Darcy yawned in Welsh.

# Chapter 4: Performance Issues

Friday. 2:30 pm. Year 3 had arrived like a natural disaster.

"Miss, why's the shop smell funny?"
"Can we touch stuff?"
"Ewan's eating a book!"
"I'm not!"
"You are!"

Mrs. Hughes, their teacher, looked apologetic. "They're excited."

Cerys had forgotten about the school visit. Completely. Until twenty-seven eight-year-olds thundered through her door. She was catastrophically hungover, surviving on Lucozade and spite.

"Right," she started. "Everyone sit—"

"I'll do it!"

Rhys appeared from behind Poetry, wielding The Gruffalo like a weapon. He'd brought the bacon sandwich as promised, stayed for tea, helped with actual inventory. Been there all day, actually. Making himself useful. Making her notice things like how he hummed when concentrating.

"You don't have to—" she began.

"I'm an actor. I act." He was already in position, chair in the center of the carpet. "Sit down, yeah? I'm going to read you the best story."

"Are you famous?" a girl with plaits asked.

"Bit famous."

"My dad says you're shit."

"Sophie!" Mrs. Hughes gasped.

"He does! He said 'that shit actor's in town.'"

Rhys's smile tightened. "Your dad's got good taste."

"I've never heard of you," a boy announced.

"Me neither."
"Me neither."
"My brother says YouTubers are better than actors."

Cerys watched Rhys shrink slightly. His fingers were white on the book.

"Should we just—" she started.

"A mouse took a stroll through the deep dark wood," he practically shouted, voice pitched weird, half-American, half-something else.

The children stared.

He tried a different voice for the fox. Cockney? Australian? Somehow both? The children exchanged looks.

"You're doing it wrong," Sophie said.

"There's no wrong way to read," Mrs. Hughes said.

"There is and he's doing it."

Rhys abandoned The Gruffalo. Grabbed another book. "What about superheroes?"

"Boring."
"For babies."
"Do you know MrBeast?"

"I was in a film with a helicopter," Rhys tried, desperate now. "And explosions."

"Was it on YouTube?"

"No, it was in cinemas—"

"Then no one saw it."

He stood up, actually started acting out an action scene. Full commitment. Sound effects. Mime-fighting invisible enemies. One child laughed. Not with him. At him.

"Is he having a breakdown?" Sophie whispered, very loudly.

"Do the helicopter bit," Cerys said suddenly.

Everyone looked at her.

"From Skyfire. Where you hang from the ladder."

"You want me to..."

"Hang from something. Be an idiot. They're eight. They like idiots."

He looked around. Spotted the heavy-duty shelving. "If I die, my manager will sue you."

"Your manager's a prick."

"Miss said prick!" Ewan screamed, delighted.

Chaos. Beautiful chaos. Rhys hauled himself up the shelving, hung from one arm, quoting his terrible dialogue: "Looks like this bird's about to fly!"

The kids screamed with laughter. He was good at physical comedy, she realized. When he wasn't trying so hard.

"Do more!"

He did the bit where his parachute didn't open. Mime-plummeting. Crashed into beanbags they used for reading corner. Children piled on top of him.

"Are you Cerys's boyfriend?" Sophie asked, sitting on his chest.

"No," they both said. Too fast. Too loud.

"Why not?"

"Because—"
"We're not—"
"He's just—"
"She doesn't—"

The children watched this car crash with interest.

"My mum says you need a boyfriend," Sophie told Cerys. "She says your eggs are getting old."

"Your mum needs to mind her business."

"She says that's impossible in Hay."

Mrs. Hughes corralled them toward the door eventually, full of apologies and promises about "discussing appropriate questions."

Rhys was sprawled in the beanbags still, sweating. "That was fucking humiliating."

"You were good with them."

"They thought I was pathetic."

"They're eight. They think everyone over twelve is pathetic."

"Do you want coffee?" He asked suddenly. "Proper coffee? Not the shit I brought yesterday?"

"I have instant."

"That's not coffee."

"It's brown and caffeinated."

"Low bar."

They sat in her office—generous term for a cupboard with a desk. Knees touching under the narrow space. He made the instant coffee wrong—water first, then powder. She didn't correct him.

"The film bombed," he said suddenly. "Skyfire. Lost the studio forty million."

"I know."

"How?"

"I have Google."

"Right." He stared at his terrible coffee. "It was my fault. The director wanted someone else. Tom Hardy, actually. But I screen-tested better. Except I can't... I can't actually act. I just do the same thing. Charming rogue with a gun. That's it. That's all I've got."

"You were in that period drama. With the bonnets."

"Charming rogue with a horse."

"The spy thing?"

"Charming rogue with gadgets."

"The one where you're a teacher?"

"Charming rogue with a gradebook." He paused. "You've seen a lot of my films."

"Insomnia."

"All of them?"

She drank her coffee to avoid answering. "You were different today. With the kids. When you stopped trying."

"I'm always trying. That's the problem. I can't stop." He rubbed his jaw. "Madeleine said being with me was exhausting. Like dating a YouTube compilation of 'actor tries too hard.'"

"That's specific."

"She was very precise about my failings. Had a spreadsheet."

"Actually?"

"Colour-coded."

Cerys laughed. He looked pleased.

"I can't believe you quoted your own line," she said.

"'This bird's about to fly' is iconic."

"It's terrible."

"I know." He shifted. Their knees pressed harder. "I've got three more films in contract. All the same. Charming rogue with various props."

"What do you want to do?"

"Theater, maybe? Something where the words matter. Shakespeare. Except I tried once and forgot my lines. Opening night. Just stood there. Two hundred people watching me say nothing."

"What did you do?"

"Took my shirt off."

"Of course you did."

"Got a standing ovation."

"For taking your shirt off?"

"It was a very good shirt removal."

She was laughing properly now. He was too. The tiny office felt warmer. His hand was on the desk, close to hers. Not touching. Almost.

Someone knocked on the door. They jumped apart like teenagers. Cerys banged her knee. Rhys spilled the terrible coffee.

"Shit—"

"Sorry—"

Mrs. Pritchard's voice: "You've left the till open, love. And that child's definitely eaten some of that book. His mum wants a discount."

Reality rushed back. The shop. The till. The real world where he was famous and she sold books for a living.

"I should go," he said.

"Okay."

Neither moved.

"I'm filming tomorrow. In Cardiff. More charming rogue business."

"Right."

"But Sunday?"

"Sunday I do the accounts."

"Sexy."

"Devastatingly."

He stood to leave. In the doorway: "I wasn't acting today. The trying too hard. That was just... me."

"I know."

"Is that worse?"

She thought about it. "No. It's better. The real thing's always better than the performance."

He looked at her for a long moment. "Yeah. It is."

After he left, she sat in the coffee-stained office, thinking about his knee against hers and how badly he'd wanted those kids to like him.

Sophie was right. He probably was having a breakdown.

She was probably having one too.
step_outputs.10:
# Chapter 5: Local Celebrity

Tuesday. 7:45 pm. The Cross Keys smelled of thirty years of spilled beer and broken dreams.

"He's not coming," Cerys said for the third time.

"He literally just texted you," Sian said, arranging their answer sheets.

"He texted 'be there in five' twenty minutes ago."

"That's how men tell time."

Gareth from the chip shop arrived with crisps. "Alright? We winning tonight?"

"We've never won," Cerys said.

"First time for everything."

"We came last when the category was 'Fish and Chips.'"

"That was rigged. Everyone knows curry sauce isn't traditional."

The pub was filling up. Quiz night was serious business. The Quizlamic Extremists had a spreadsheet. Norfolk and Chance brought reference books. The Smarty Pints were already drunk.

"Sorry, sorry—" Rhys burst through the door, rain-soaked, still in costume. "Fucking M4. Fucking sheep. Fucking—is that a sword?"

He was wearing some sort of period military jacket. Brass buttons. Epaulettes. Tight trousers that were—

"It's from the film," he said, catching her stare. "Didn't have time to change."

"You look like a stripper," Gareth said cheerfully. "Naval theme?"

"I'm playing Nelson."

"Nelson had one arm."

"Before that."

"Still a stripper outfit."

Rhys dropped into the seat beside Cerys. Too close. The jacket was still damp. He smelled like hairspray and fake smoke.

"This is Gareth," Cerys said. "He runs the chippy."

"Best battered sausage in Wales," Gareth said.

"Doubt it," Sian muttered.

"And this is my sister—"

"Oh my God, you're actually him," Sian said. "I mean—hello. Normal hello. Not weird."

"That was weird, Sian."

"Shut up."

Derek the quizmaster tapped the microphone. "Right, you lot. Phones away. No Wikipedia, Norfolk—I can see you."

"Team name?" Rhys whispered.

"Quiz Pro Quo," Cerys said.

"That's terrible."

"It was that or Quizteama Aguilera."

"Also terrible."

"You're very judgmental for someone dressed like Captain Pugwash."

"Round one," Derek boomed. "Geography."

They were catastrophically bad. Rhys thought Uruguay was in Europe. Gareth insisted France was landlocked. Sian spelled Reykjavik with a Q.

"We're losing to the team that's just three dogs in a trenchcoat," Cerys hissed.

"Those aren't dogs," Gareth said. "That's the Williams brothers."

"Round two. Science."

Worse. Rhys drew a penis instead of a molecule. Claimed it was "abstract chemistry."

"That's not a thing."

"Could be."

His hand found her knee under the table. Just resting there. Warm through her jeans. She didn't move away. Didn't acknowledge it. The quiz continued around this fixed point of contact.

"Round three. Film and Television."

Rhys came alive. "Finally. My moment."

"Name the film: 'I'll be back.'"

"Terminator," he whispered, writing fast.

"Everyone knows that," Gareth said.

"Which Terminator?"

"Does it matter?"

"There are six."

"Six?"

"And a TV series."

He was right about everything. Directors, years, obscure sequels. His hand stayed on her knee, thumb moving slightly when he got excited about an answer.

"You're Rain Man for shit films," Sian said, impressed.

"It's my job."

"Which film won Best Picture in 1994?"

"Forrest Gump," Rhys said. "Beat Pulp Fiction. Travesty."

"You weren't even born."

"I was eight."

"You're old," Gareth said.

"Thirty-six isn't old."

"It's dad age."

"Jesus Christ."

"Round four. History."

They gave up. Rhys ordered shots. Then more shots. His hand moved higher on her thigh. Not suggestively. Absently. Like he'd forgotten it was there.

"Which Welsh prince—"

"Owain Glyndŵr," Cerys said automatically.

"You don't know the question."

"It's always Owain Glyndŵr."

She was right.

"We should get points for that," Rhys told Derek, too loud. "Psychic points."

"Sit down, Nelson."

"I have both arms!"

"Not for long if you keep shouting."

They came second to last. Beat only by the Williams brothers who might have been dogs.

Outside, unexpected rain. Gareth ran for his van. Sian suddenly remembered an urgent appointment. "Very urgent. Elsewhere. Bye!"

Subtle as bricks, her sister.

They stood under the pub's leaking awning. Rhys swayed slightly.

"You're pissed," Cerys observed.

"Bit pissed. Medium pissed."

"Thursday pissed?"

"Tuesday pissed. Different scale." He turned to face her. Too close. "I should tell you something."

"Okay."

"I'm complicated."

"I know."

"No, like... properly complicated. Ex-girlfriend, career shit, managers, the whole... thing."

"I know."

"I live in Los Angeles."

"I know."

"I'm probably having some sort of breakdown."

"Definitely."

He laughed. Leaned closer. "You make me want to be less complicated."

"That's a line from your Venice film."

"Is it? Fuck. See? I can't even... I'm not good at real things."

"This feels real."

"Does it?"

He was going to kiss her. She could see it happening—the lean, the intent. She turned her head at the last second. His lips hit her ear. Warm breath, awkward angle.

"Sorry—"

"I don't do complicated," she said to his shoulder.

"I'm not complicated."

She laughed. Couldn't help it. "You're literally famous. That's the definition of complicated."

"Only a bit famous."

"You've been papped three times this week."

"Twice. The third was just someone really interested in my coffee order."

They stood there. Not kissing. Her ear was warm where his mouth had missed. His hand found hers. Their fingers didn't quite interlace. Just tangled. Confused.

"I want to kiss you," he said. "Properly. Not... ear kissing."

"Ear kissing isn't a thing."

"Could be. New trend."

"Started by you?"

"I'm an innovator."

An old man emerged from the pub, looked at them. "You're that actor. The one who can't act."

"That's me," Rhys agreed.

"Thought so. You're better looking in person."

"Thanks?"

"Still can't act though."

He wandered off. They laughed. Tension broke and reformed differently.

"Walk me home?" Cerys asked.

"Yes. Good. Walking."

They walked badly. Rhys kept listing left in his Nelson jacket. She kept not quite taking his arm. The rain was persistent, Welsh rain, more mist than drops.

At her shop door, he said, "I want to do this right."

"There's no right."

"Less wrong then."

"That's achievable."

"Sunday?"

"I told you, accounts."

"Monday?"

"Stock take."

"Tuesday?"

"You're very available for someone famous."

"I'm method acting unemployment."

She unlocked the door. Paused. "Tuesday maybe."

"Maybe?"

"Probably maybe."

He grinned. Swayed. Caught himself on the doorframe. "I should go before I fall over."

"Very sexy."

"I'm extremely sexy. The coat says so."

"Go home, Nelson."

"I don't have a home. Just hotels."

It was meant to be flippant. Came out sad. They both heard it.

"Tuesday," she said firmly.

"Tuesday."

He did a terrible salute, walked backward into a lamppost, recovered with surprising grace.

She watched him weave down the street, period costume dark with rain. Her ear was still warm. Her thigh still felt his hand.

Inside, Mr. Darcy gave her a look.

"Shut up," she told him.

She made cheese on toast at midnight, checked her phone forty times. No message.

Then at 1 AM: "home save. SAFE. home safe. got an uber. driver recognized me. gave me two stars. fair tbh."

Then: "your ear smells nice."

Then: "weird sorry."

She turned off her phone. Turned it back on. Typed: "Tuesday."

Deleted it. Typed it again. Sent it.

Three dots immediately.

"tuesday tuesday tuesday tuesday"

"Go to sleep."

"can't. wearing nelson coat to bed. buttons complicated."

"That's not my problem."

"could be though"

She turned the phone definitely off. Smiled at her ceiling like an idiot.

Tuesday.

# Chapter 6: Damage Control

Wednesday. 8:47 am.

Cerys woke to thirty-seven WhatsApp messages and a headache where her dignity used to be.

"LOOK AT THE DAILY MAIL"
"OH MY GOD"
"MUM KNOWS"
"CHECK TWITTER"
"IS THAT HIS TONGUE???"

She sat up too fast. Rhys's arm fell off her waist. He was wearing her uncle's Wales rugby shirt and nothing else, face mashed into her pillow, snoring.

Her phone rang. Unknown number.

"Don't answer that," he mumbled into cotton.

She answered it.

"IS THIS CERYS DAVIES?"

"Who's—"

"DAILY MAIL. CAN YOU CONFIRM—"

She hung up. It rang immediately.

"Turn it off," Rhys said, pulling a pillow over his head.

She opened Twitter. Mistake. The photos were everywhere. Him at the pub in that ridiculous Nelson jacket. Her laughing. His hand on her thigh (when did that happen?). Then grainier ones—them against her fridge, her hands in his hair, his mouth on her neck.

"Fuck."

"Hmm?"

"We're on the internet."

He sat up, shirt riding up. Grabbed her phone. "Oh."

"Oh? That's it?"

His phone started ringing. Simon's name flashing.

"Don't—" she started.

He answered. She could hear shouting from across the room.

"WHAT THE FUCK WERE YOU THINKING?"

"Simon—"

"WELSH BOOKSHOP OWNER? ARE YOU TAKING THE PISS?"

"She has a name—"

"I DON'T CARE IF SHE'S PRINCESS FUCKING DIANA—"

Rhys held the phone away from his ear. "He's upset."

"I got that."

"THIS IS PERFECT!" Simon's voice changed. "Local girl, wholesome, salt of the earth—"

"I'm right here," Cerys said loudly.

"Is that her? Brilliant. Love the accent. Very authentic."

"I'm hanging up," Rhys said.

"DON'T YOU DARE—"

He hung up. Turned off his phone. "Sorry."

"He seems nice."

"He's actually worse in person."

She scrolled through the photos. They looked... intimate. Happy. Her hand on his chest. Him looking at her like—

"I look drunk," she said.

"You were drunk."

"I wasn't that drunk."

"You tried to speak Welsh."

"I can speak Welsh."

"You told me my arse was like two eggs in a hanky."

"That's... that's not what I said."

"It's what it sounded like."

Someone knocked on the door downstairs. Then again. Harder.

"Customers?" he asked hopefully.

She checked the shop's Instagram. Forty-three messages. Someone had found her personal Facebook. Her mum's Facebook.

"Oh God, my mum."

Her phone rang. Mum.

"Don't—"

She answered. Had to.

"Cerys Ann Davies."

"Mum—"

"Mrs. Henderson saw the photographs."

"Which Mrs. Henderson?"

"All of them. They have a WhatsApp group."

Rhys was getting dressed, hopping into his period military trousers. Still no pants, she noticed. Then felt weird for noticing.

"I have to go, Mum."

"Sian says he's there."

"Sian doesn't know anything."

"She's outside."

Cerys looked out the window. Sian waved from the street, holding coffee and what looked like crisis pastries.

"I'm not opening the shop today," Cerys decided.

"You have to open. It's Wednesday."

"Nobody cares if a bookshop's closed on Wednesday."

More knocking. Rhys peered out. "There's quite a lot of people."

She looked. Photographers. Actual customers. Mrs. Pritchard with her Wednesday crime novel.

"Fuck."

"We could go out the back?" he suggested.

"That's an alley. With bins."

"Romantic."

"This isn't funny."

"It's a bit funny." He was buttoning the jacket wrong. Gave up. "Look, we just... go down. Act normal."

"Normal? You're in a Nelson costume doing the walk of shame."

"It's not shame. It's... historical romance."

"That's worse."

Her phone buzzed. The shop phone started ringing downstairs.

"I fucked it up," he said suddenly. "This is my fault."

"It takes two people to—"

"No, I mean... I knew they were following me. Since Sunday. Should have been more careful."

"You knew?"

"They always are. Part of the... thing."

She stared at him. "So you knew when you came to the pub?"

"Yes."

"When you put your hand on my leg?"

"I wasn't thinking about—"

"When you came back here?"

"Cerys—"

She stood up. Started dressing properly. Angrily. "Right. Got it."

"It's not like that."

"Bit like that though."

"I didn't plan this."

"Just happened to be photographed. Just happened to be good PR."

"That's not—" His phone was ringing again, silent but insistent. "I don't care about PR."

"Your manager does."

"He's a prick."

"He's your prick."

"That sounds wrong."

"You know what I mean."

They stood there, half-dressed, phones screaming, the world pressing in through thin windows.

"I like you," he said. "Actually like you. Not PR like. Real like."

"That's very articulate."

"I'm not good at this."

"At what?"

"Real things. Actual feelings. Without scripts."

Someone got the shop door open downstairs. The bell jangled.

"Cerys?" Mrs. Pritchard's voice. "You've got a situation out here, love."

"Fuck." She headed down. He followed.

The shop was chaos. People with cameras. Actual customers confused by the fuss. Mrs. Pritchard holding court by Crime, telling someone Rhys was "adequate at best."

"We're closed," Cerys announced.

"It's Wednesday," Mrs. Pritchard said.

"Emergency closure."

"Gas leak," Rhys added helpfully.

"You don't smell gas," someone said.

"Silent gas," he tried.

"That's not a thing."

Cerys physically herded people out. Locked the door. Pulled down the blind that had never worked properly and still didn't.

"Well," Mrs. Pritchard said, still inside somehow. "You've made a mess of this."

"Thanks for the insight."

"He's still too tall for you."

"I'm right here," Rhys said.

"I know, love. Hard to miss in that outfit."

She left through the back, tutting.

They stood in the empty shop. Phones still ringing. Someone knocked again.

"I should go," he said.

"Yeah."

"I'll fix this."

"Sure."

"Cerys—"

"Just go."

He went to the back door. Stopped. "Last night was—"

"A mistake?"

"I was going to say nice."

"Nice?"

"Really nice."

After he left, she sat on the floor behind the counter. The shop felt wrong. Invaded.

Her phone buzzed. Sian: "Mum wants you for dinner. Bringing wine. And questions. So many questions."

Then: "He looked proper fit in that jacket though."

Then: "Your ear thing made the Mail Online."

She turned off her phone. Made a sandwich. Cheese and pickle. It tasted like disappointment.

At 4pm, she heard someone at the back door. Rhys, holding terrible coffee and good wine.

"Silent gas emergency over?"

"Apparently."

They sat on her back steps. Drank wine from mugs. He'd changed into normal clothes. Still looked absurd.

"My ex called me pathetic," he said. "For hiding in Wales with my 'shop girl.'"

"Shop girl?"

"Her words."

"Charming."

"She's actually quite charming. When she's not being a massive—"

His phone rang. He threw it into the bins.

"That was dramatic."

"I'm an actor. We're dramatic."

"You can't act though."

"Exactly. So I'm just dramatic for no reason."

She laughed despite herself. He shifted closer.

"Don't," she said.

"I'm not."

"You are."

His hand found hers. "This is just hand stuff. Not complicated."

"Everything with you is complicated."

"My left hand isn't complicated. Very straightforward, my left hand."

Her phone buzzed. Sian: "MUM KNOWS ABOUT THE TONGUE THING."

"Kill me," Cerys said.

"That seems extreme."

"My mother knows about your tongue."

"My tongue's famous. It's been in films."

"Saying words."

"Sometimes."

They sat there, holding hands like teenagers, the world shouting through their phones.

"I don't know how to do this," she said.

"Neither do I."

"You literally date actresses."

"Dated. Past tense. And that was just... posing next to each other at things."

"That's not dating?"

"Apparently not."

The sun was setting. The bins smelled terrible. His thumb was doing something devastating to her palm.

"Tuesday seems very far away now," she said.

"We missed Tuesday. It's Wednesday."

"I meant next Tuesday."

"That's eight Tuesdays away."

"That's not how days work."

"Feels like it."

Someone knocked on the front door again. They ignored it.

"We could try this," she said carefully. "But I don't know how."

"Try what?"

"Whatever this is."

"Hand stuff?"

"Stop calling it that."

He turned to face her properly. Nelson jacket gone but something in his eyes that made her stomach flip.

"We could just... see what happens," he said.

"That's not a plan."

"I don't have a plan. My plans involve explosions and terrible dialogue."

"And gondolas."

"One gondola. Singular."

She kissed him. Badly. Noses bumped. He laughed into her mouth.

"Smooth," he said.

"Shut up."

"Make me."

"That's definitely from one of your films."

"Probably."

They kissed better the second time. Until her phone rang. Mum again.

"Dinner," Cerys sighed. "In an hour."

"I should come."

"That's a terrible idea."

"I'm good with parents."

"No you're not."

"I'm not. But I should come anyway."

"You'll make it worse."

"Definitely. But in a charming way."

She looked at him. Period costume ruined, hair ridiculous, trying so hard it hurt to watch.

"Fine. But you're not wearing that."

"I've got a shirt in the bins now."

"Why—never mind."

They stood to go inside. He stopped her.

"This is probably a disaster," he said.

"Definitely."

"But a good disaster?"

"That's not a thing."

"Could be."

Her phone buzzed. Sian: "MUM'S MADE WELSH CAKES. SHE ONLY DOES THAT FOR PROPER BOYFRIENDS."

"Oh God," Cerys said.

"What?"

"Welsh cakes."

"Is that code?"

"It's worse. It's hope."

# Chapter 7: Terrible Timing

Saturday morning. 7:23 am.

Rhys was destroying her kitchen.

"That's not how you make scrambled eggs," Cerys said from the doorway.

"It's exactly how you make them." He was wearing her uncle Dai's rugby shirt. Nothing else. Wielding a spatula like a weapon.

"The pan's too hot."

"It's fine."

"They're turning grey."

"That's the desired color."

"Of eggs?"

"My eggs."

She watched him scrape what looked like rubber into two bowls. He'd used all six eggs. The milk was out, going warm. Sugar in the salt cellar—she could tell from here.

"Delicious," she lied, taking a bite.

"You don't have to eat it."

"No, it's... interesting."

"Interesting?"

"Textured."

They were being so careful. Like the last three days hadn't happened. Like her mother hadn't interrogated him over Welsh cakes. Like they hadn't spent Thursday and Friday hiding in her flat, phones off, pretending the world didn't exist.

"My flight's at noon," he said to his eggs.

"Right."

"From Bristol."

"That's good. Close."

"Two hours."

"Better leave at nine then."

"Half nine maybe."

Neither of them looked at each other. The eggs sat between them, accusatory.

Someone knocked. They both froze.

"It's seven in the morning," she said.

"Maybe they'll go away."

More knocking. Then: "I know you're in there. I can smell burning."

Her mother.

"Fuck," they said together.

"I'll get dressed," he said, but her mother was already using her spare key.

"Cerys? I've brought casserole for—oh."

She stopped in the doorway. Rhys in the rugby shirt. Cerys in his t-shirt from yesterday. The murdered eggs.

"Morning, Mrs. Davies," Rhys said, pulling the shirt down. It didn't help.

"That's Dai's shirt."

"I... yes."

"He died in that shirt."

"Mum, he didn't die in it."

"Watching rugby. Same thing." She set down a casserole dish. Enormous. "Cawl. For your drive."

"That's kind," Rhys said.

"It's not for you. You probably don't eat real food." She looked at the eggs. "I can see that."

"Mum—"

"Mrs. Henderson saw you yesterday. In Tesco. Buying wine."

"People buy wine."

"Three bottles."

"It was on offer."

"Hmm." She was opening cupboards now. Tutting. "No proper tea. What's this? Chamomile? Are you ill?"

"It's calming," Rhys said.

"Are you nervous?"

"Currently? Yes."

She turned to really look at him. "You're leaving."

Not a question.

"I have work. In LA. Reshoots."

"Reshoots," she repeated. Like he'd said 'puppy murdering.'

"I'll be back."

"When?"

"Soon."

"Soon." She looked at Cerys. "He says soon."

"I heard."

"Men always say soon."

"Mum—"

"Your father said soon. Went to get milk. Came back three days later from Ibiza."

"That was different—"

"Had a sunburn. In February."

Rhys was backing toward the bedroom. "I should... trousers."

He fled.

Her mother sat down. "He seems nice."

"Mum—"

"Bit stupid. But nice."

"He's not stupid."

"He put sugar in your salt."

"How do you—never mind."

"His films are terrible."

"I know."

"Sian made me watch one. The gondola one."

"Oh God."

"Ninety minutes I'll never get back."

They sat in silence. Could hear Rhys dropping things in the bedroom.

"You like him," her mother said.

"It's complicated."

"It's not. You like him or you don't."

"He lives in Los Angeles."

"And?"

"And I live here."

"Planes exist."

"It's not that simple."

"It never is with you."

Rhys emerged, dressed. Designer jeans that cost more than her monthly shop rent. The t-shirt she'd pulled off him yesterday.

"I should go," he said.

It was only 7:45.

"Traffic," he added.

"On Saturday?"

"Sheep. Possibly."

Her mother stood. "I'll leave you to it." At the door: "That casserole's for four people. Don't let him have any."

She left. They stood in the kitchen, three feet and a lifetime apart.

"Your mum's terrifying."

"She likes you."

"How can you tell?"

"She didn't mention your height."

He smiled. Weak but real. "I do have to go."

"I know."

"I'll call."

"Time difference."

"Text then."

"Yeah."

They were terrible at this. Both moving toward each other, stopping, starting again.

"This is stupid," he said. "I'm coming back. Two weeks max."

"Okay."

"Maybe three."

"Right."

"There's awards season stuff. But after—"

"It's fine."

"It's not fine. This isn't—" He stopped. Started again. "I want this to be something."

"It is something."

"Something more than me fucking off to LA after three days."

"Four days."

"Does Wednesday count?"

"Half a Wednesday."

"So three and a half days."

They were closer now. She could smell her shower gel on his skin.

"This is going to be complicated," she said.

"I know."

"Properly complicated. Not rom-com complicated."

"I know."

"I don't know if I can do complicated."

He touched her face. Thumb on her cheekbone. "We could try?"

"That's not a plan."

"I don't have a plan."

"You never have plans."

"I had one. Come to Wales, hide from photographers, meet nobody, leave."

"How'd that work out?"

"Fucking terribly."

She kissed him. He tasted like terrible eggs and possibility.

"I have to go," he said against her mouth.

"You said that."

"Still true."

But they were moving backward, toward the bedroom. Her mother's casserole cooling on the counter.

"You'll miss your flight."

"There's others."

"Your manager—"

"Can fuck off."

Later—much later—he was actually leaving. 11:47. Definitely missing the flight.

"There's a two-thirty," he said, throwing things in a bag.

"You should go."

"Going."

"Now."

"Yes."

But he was kissing her again. Against the door. Her leg around his hip.

"This is ridiculous," she breathed.

"Very."

A car horn outside. His Uber.

"Fuck."

He grabbed his bag. Stopped. "I'm coming back."

"Okay."

"I am."

"I know."

He didn't look convinced. Neither was she.

At the door: "Keep the shirt."

"The one your ex gave you?"

"It looks better on you."

"Everything looks better on me."

"True."

The horn again.

"Go."

He went. Came back. Kissed her hard. Left again.

She watched from the window. He looked up, waved. Got in the car. Gone.

The flat felt wrong. Too quiet. Smelled like him—that stupid cologne and something underneath that was just Rhys.

Her phone buzzed. Him: "Made the 2:30."

Then: "Miss you already."

Then: "That's pathetic sorry."

Then: "But true."

She typed back: "The eggs were shit."

"I know."

"Really shit."

"The worst eggs."

"Come back and make better ones."

"Soon."

That word. Soon. Her mother was right about soon.

She wrapped herself in Uncle Dai's rugby shirt. It smelled like complicated.

At 4am her phone lit up: "Landed. LA is terrible. Full of Americans."

She smiled in the dark. Typed: "Go to sleep."

"Can't. Bed's too big."

"That's a real problem. Definitely worth complaining about."

"Your sarcasm translates perfectly to text."

"It's a gift."

"I left my good jacket."

"I know."

"On purpose."

"I know."

"So I have to come back."

"Your jacket's not going anywhere."

"Good."

Three dots appeared. Disappeared. Appeared again.

"This is something," he wrote finally. "Whatever this is. It's something."

She stared at the message. Didn't know what to say. The dots appeared on his end again.

"Unless you don't want—"

She typed quickly: "It's something."

"Good something?"

"Complicated something."

"I can do complicated."

"No you can't."

"I can try."

"That's not the same."

"It's what I've got."

She pulled his shirt tighter. Breathed him in. Typed: "It's enough."

For now, she didn't add.

"Soon," he wrote.

That fucking word.

"Soon," she replied anyway.

Because what else was there to say? He was there and she was here and that was the truth of it. Everything else was just time zones and terrible eggs and hoping soon meant something more than maybe.

Her mother had left the casserole.

She ate all of it.

# Chapter 8: Ongoing Situation

Six weeks later. Tuesday. 10:47 am.

"He's not coming back," Mrs. Pritchard said, inspecting crime novels.

"I didn't say he was."

"You've checked your phone twelve times since I walked in."

"I'm expecting a delivery."

"At 3am Los Angeles time?"

Cerys didn't answer. The shop was busy—had been since the photos. Rhys Morgan fans buying books they'd never read, asking questions she wouldn't answer.

"That actor off the telly was in here, wasn't he?"
"Did he sign anything?"
"Are you the girlfriend?"
"Is it true about the ear thing?"

She'd hired help. Lowri, seventeen, A-levels pending, competent with Instagram. Currently serving a woman buying eight copies of Skyfire: The Novel (it existed, somehow).

"For my book club," the woman lied.

Her phone buzzed. Not him. Never him at reasonable hours.

Gareth: "Pub tonight?"

She'd been sort-of seeing Gareth. If seeing meant letting him buy her drinks while she talked about anything except what they weren't doing.

"Maybe," she replied.

"That's a no then."

It was.

Her phone lit up. 2:47 am LA time. He was drunk again. Or filming nights. Or both.

"Can't sleep. Director's a prick. Catering is shit. Miss proper tea."

She typed: "Go to bed."

"Can't. Too many thoughts."

"Think quieter."

"Harsh."

"Accurate."

Three dots. Then: "Madeleine's here."

Her stomach dropped. She typed: "Okay."

"Not like that. Same film. She plays my wife."

"Method acting?"

"Fuck off."

"Professional."

"She says hi."

"She doesn't."

"She doesn't."

Cerys put the phone down. Picked it up. Put it down again.

"He's definitely not coming back," Mrs. Pritchard observed.

"Thank you for your input."

"I'm just saying. Men who leave don't usually—"

The door chimed. Cerys looked up from habit, heart doing that stupid hopeful thing.

Not him. Another fan.

"Excuse me, are you the one from the photos?"

"No."

"You look like—"

"I get that a lot."

The woman bought three bookmarks and left, disappointed.

Her phone: "Not like that with M. Promise."

Then: "Though she did suggest drinks."

Then: "I said no."

Then: "Obviously."

Then: "Why aren't you answering?"

Then: "Time difference. Sorry. You're probably asleep."

She wasn't asleep. Hadn't been sleeping properly for weeks. Her bed still smelled faintly of his cologne, which was pathetic.

She typed: "I'm awake."

Immediately: "Sorry. Shit. Did I wake you?"

"No. Shop's open."

"Right. Tuesday."

"Every day's Tuesday."

"That's depressing."

"Accurate."

"I miss Tuesdays."

"You're being weird."

"I'm always weird."

"Weirder than normal."

"Director wants me to be 'more authentic.' I don't know what that means."

"It means act better."

"Helpful. Thanks."

"You asked."

"I didn't."

"Implicitly."

"Using big words now?"

"Always did. You weren't paying attention."

"I was distracted by your ear."

"Still weird about the ear thing."

"It's a nice ear."

"All ears look the same."

"Yours doesn't."

"Anatomically impossible."

"Your ear is special."

"You're drunk."

"Bit drunk."

"Wednesday drunk?"

"Every day drunk. LA's boring."

"You live in LA."

"I exist in LA. Different thing."

Lowri was looking at her. "You alright? You're making faces at your phone."

"I'm fine."

"Is it him?"

"No."

"Your face says yes."

"My face is wrong."

Her phone rang. She almost dropped it. Video call.

"Don't answer that," Lowri said. "You look terrible."

She answered anyway.

He looked worse. Tired. Thinner. Ridiculous tan from filming.

"Hi," he said.

"Hi."

"You look good."

"I look like shit."

"Good shit."

"That's not a compliment."

"It is how I mean it."

They stared at each other through pixels and distance.

"I should go," he said.

"Okay."

"Early call tomorrow."

"Right."

"Cerys—"

"It's fine."

"It's not fine."

"It is what it is."

"I hate that phrase."

"Doesn't make it wrong."

He rubbed his jaw. That nervous tell. "Two more weeks. Then I'm back."

"You said that four weeks ago."

"I know."

"And two weeks before that."

"I know."

"So."

"So I'm shit at time."

"And promises."

"That too."

Someone was trying to get his attention off-screen. He waved them away.

"I have to go."

"You said."

"I'm coming back."

"Okay."

"I am."

"I know."

"You don't believe me."

She didn't. "I do."

"Liar."

"Actor."

"Same thing."

They smiled at each other sadly.

"Tuesday?" he said.

"It's always Tuesday."

He hung up. She stood there, holding her phone like an idiot.

"He's definitely coming back," Lowri said.

"No he's not."

"That was the face of a man who's coming back."

"That was pixels arranged to look like a face."

"You're very cynical for someone in love."

"I'm not—"

The door chimed.

She didn't look up. Couldn't handle another fan, another question, another—

"Black coffee."

She froze.

He was standing there. Real. Solid. Holding terrible coffee from the place that didn't do tea. Messenger bag over his shoulder. Same jacket he'd left behind but rumpled like he'd slept in it.

"You don't drink coffee," he said.

"You're supposed to be in LA."

"I was. Now I'm not."

"It's Tuesday."

"I know."

"You said two weeks."

"I lied."

They stood there. The shop between them. Lowri recording on her phone.

"Put that away," Cerys said.

"This is content gold."

"Now."

Lowri sulked off to Poetry.

"I quit," Rhys said.

"Quit what?"

"The film. The next film. The films after that."

"You can't quit."

"I did. Simon screamed for forty minutes. I put him on speaker and made a sandwich."

"That's..."

"Stupid? Impulsive? Career suicide?"

"I was going to say brave."

"It's not. It's terrifying."

He moved closer. She stayed behind the counter. Safe distance.

"Why?" she asked.

"You know why."

"I don't."

"Yes you do."

"I need you to say it."

He put the coffee down. Ran his hands through his hair. It was longer. Needed cutting.

"I can't sleep. I can't eat. I definitely can't act, which was already a problem. I spent three hours yesterday looking at that photo of your ear."

"Still weird."

"I know. But I don't care. I'm sick of LA. I'm sick of films where I take my shirt off. I'm sick of not being here."

"Here's just a shop."

"You know what I mean."

"I don't do big gestures."

"This isn't a gesture. Gestures are for films. This is just... turning up."

"On a Tuesday."

"You like Tuesdays."

"I tolerate Tuesdays."

"You said it's always Tuesday."

"That was existential despair."

"Sounded like romance."

"You're bad at reading signals."

"Terrible."

They were close now. When did that happen?

"I'm seeing Gareth," she said.

"No you're not."

"I could be."

"He's married. To Terry. They have two kids."

"Oh."

"You didn't know?"

"I don't pay attention."

"You should."

"You're one to talk."

"What's that mean?"

She grabbed his jacket. Pulled him across the counter. Kissed him hard. He tasted like airplane coffee and bad decisions.

"I can't move to LA," she said against his mouth.

"I'm not asking you to."

"I won't follow you to locations."

"Don't want you to."

"I eat the same sandwich every day."

"I know."

"I'm boring."

"You're not."

"I am. Deeply boring."

"Good. I need boring."

Mrs. Pritchard coughed. "You're blocking the crime novels."

They jumped apart.

"You're back then," she said to Rhys.

"Apparently."

"Still too tall."

"Working on it."

She grabbed her book and left. "Give him the friends and family discount, Cerys."

"We don't have—"

"Twenty percent."

The door chimed.

"So," Rhys said.

"So."

"I need somewhere to stay."

"Hotels exist."

"I was thinking somewhere more... vertical."

"That's not how you use that word."

"Above a shop. Vertical."

"That's still wrong."

"Can I stay or not?"

"I'll think about it."

"How long?"

"Tuesday."

"It's Tuesday now."

"Next Tuesday."

"That's seven Tuesdays away."

"Still not how days work."

He kissed her again. In the middle of the shop. In front of Lowri and three customers and Mr. Darcy.

"I'm not good at this," he said.

"I know."

"But I want to try."

"That might not be enough."

"I know."

"This could go really badly."

"Probably will."

"You're supposed to argue."

"I'm done pretending things are different than they are."

She looked at him. Exhausted. Earnest. Slightly manic from the flight.

"You can't make eggs."

"I can learn."

"You leave wet towels everywhere."

"I'll stop."

"You quote your own films."

"That's charming."

"It's really not."

"I'll stop that too."

"You won't."

"I won't."

The shop phone rang. She ignored it.

"This is just trying," she said. "Not promising."

"I know."

"I might hate you in a week."

"Fair."

"You might get bored."

"Never."

"You don't know that."

"I know I've been not-here for six weeks and it's been shit."

"That's not the same as being here being good."

"It's a start."

She wanted to argue. To be sensible. To protect herself from whatever disaster this would become.

Instead she said, "Tea. Three sugars."

"What?"

"If you're staying, you need to learn tea."

"Okay."

"And the right way to make eggs."

"Arguable."

"And Welsh. Proper Welsh."

"That seems excessive."

"Those are the terms."

"What do I get?"

"Me. Tolerating you. Maybe."

"For how long?"

"Tuesday."

"Which Tuesday?"

"All the Tuesdays. If you're lucky."

He grinned. That stupid Hollywood grin that shouldn't work but did.

"I can do Tuesdays."

The door chimed. Another customer. Another fan probably.

Life went on. The shop stayed open. He stayed or he didn't. It worked or it didn't.

But for now, for this Tuesday, he was here. Making terrible coffee. Quoting his own films. Being real in a way that had nothing to do with cameras.

Maybe that was enough.

Maybe it wasn't.

The shop was open 9 to 5:30, closed Sundays.

Everything else was negotiable.

chapter_raw_judge_text.1:
[Analysis]

This is a genuinely accomplished piece of comic-romantic writing that executes its brief with considerable skill. Let me work through the key elements.

**Characterization:** Both characters are rendered with impressive fidelity to their profiles. Rhys's desperate need to be liked manifests in specific, embarrassing ways—quoting Yeats incorrectly (then accepting the wrong correction), grabbing the shelf too eagerly, the Superman ringtone he winces at. His voice shifts when he takes the phone call ("smoother, more American somehow") is a sharp, economical detail. Cerys's internal resistance while being quietly charmed is handled with restraint—the broken card machine that isn't broken, checking the jacket for his number. These are character-consistent choices that show rather than tell.

**Dialogue:** The dialogue is the chapter's strongest element. It has genuine rhythm and specificity. "That's volume three of a history of Welsh mining." / "I love mining. History of it. Especially Welsh." is a perfect comic exchange. Mrs. Pritchard's intrusion is well-timed and her voice is distinct. The Welsh language exchange—where he accepts a correction to a wrong answer—is clever and economical. The dialogue avoids the "perfect banter" trap the plan warned against; lines misfire, people talk past each other.

**Craft and Technique:** The physical comedy is well-staged without becoming slapstick. The forearms observation ("Like someone had designed them specifically to make sleeves look restrictive") is the chapter's one slightly overworked line—it's trying a bit hard—but it's not egregious. The ending with the jacket is earned and appropriately ambiguous. The chapter avoids purple prose almost entirely.

**Following the Plan:** The chapter follows its plan closely: the display knocked over, the Welsh language exchange, Mrs. Pritchard's appearance, the forearms moment, the jacket left behind. The £73 figure from the plan appears. The plan's goal of "genuine awkwardness" is achieved.

**Minor Weaknesses:** The "forearms were ridiculous. Like someone had designed them specifically" line is the closest thing to forced metaphor, and it's mild. The Yeats/Wordsworth gag is slightly confusing—she says it's Wordsworth when it's actually Yeats, and he accepts this, which is the joke, but the narration's "It wasn't. It was Yeats" clarification is necessary but slightly clunky. The chapter is perhaps a touch long for what it accomplishes, though it doesn't drag badly.

**Overall:** This is confident, well-executed romantic comedy writing with genuine wit, distinct voices, and characters who feel real rather than constructed. It earns its lightness without becoming frothy, and the chemistry is established through behavior rather than declaration.

[Analysis]

Chapter 7 of this piece is a strong execution of the plan's intentions, with some notable strengths and a few weaknesses worth examining critically.

**Dialogue and Voice:** The dialogue is genuinely accomplished. The characters speak in distinct, recognizable voices that feel consistent with their profiles. Rhys's deflections ("Traffic. Sheep. Possibly.") and his desperate need to reassure ("I'm coming back. Two weeks max. Maybe three.") ring true to his character. Cerys's clipped responses and dry corrections ("Four days." / "Half a Wednesday.") feel authentic to her established voice. The late-night texting sequence is particularly well-handled—the rhythm of short messages, the dots appearing and disappearing, the vulnerability breaking through ("That's pathetic sorry") all feel earned and real.

**The Mother Scene:** Mrs. Davies's arrival is the chapter's comic highlight and is executed with genuine skill. "He died in that shirt." / "Mum, he didn't die in it." / "Watching rugby. Same thing." This is sharp, specific, and funny without being forced. The mother's oblique commentary on men and "soon" (the Ibiza anecdote) is both funny and thematically resonant without being heavy-handed.

**Emotional Authenticity:** The chapter captures the specific awkwardness of a morning-after departure with real precision. The "being so careful" quality, the eggs as displacement activity, the inability to have the real conversation—these feel observed rather than invented. The ending, with Cerys eating the casserole alone, is quietly effective.

**Weaknesses:**

The phrase "tasted like terrible eggs and possibility" is the chapter's most egregious misstep—a forced poetic leap that undermines the otherwise grounded prose. It's exactly the kind of reach for profundity that the piece otherwise wisely avoids.

"It smelled like complicated" is similarly strained—the personification of an abstract concept as a smell is trying too hard for a chapter that earns its emotional beats through specificity rather than metaphor.

The chapter follows its plan faithfully: the mother's arrival, the excruciatingly polite conversation, the "I'll be back / soon" exchange, the missed flight, the late-night texts. The plan's note about "desperate and a bit sad" sex is handled with appropriate discretion (implied rather than shown), which is the right call.

**Consistency with Profiles:** Rhys quoting his departure time obsessively, the designer jeans detail, leaving his jacket on purpose—all consistent. Cerys's defensive practicality, her mother's intrusive warmth, the rugby shirt detail (Uncle Dai) adding specificity—all well-handled.

**Minor Issues:** The chapter occasionally tells us what we can already see ("They were being so careful"). The "three feet and a lifetime apart" line is a cliché. The piece is slightly repetitive in the departure sequence—he leaves, comes back, leaves again—though this is intentional and mostly works.

Overall, this is a confident, well-executed chapter that delivers on its plan's promises. The dialogue is its strongest element, the emotional beats are earned, and the specific details (the casserole, the rugby shirt, the sugar in the salt cellar) do real work. The occasional reach for poetic language is the main weakness in an otherwise grounded piece.

The "hand stuff" running gag is a good example of comedy that emerges organically from character rather than being imposed.

The chapter follows its plan faithfully. The photos surfacing, Simon's call, Mrs. Pritchard's imperious commentary, the back steps wine scene, the dinner invitation—all present and executed with reasonable fidelity to the blueprint. The "MUM KNOWS ABOUT THE TONGUE THING" escalation through Sian's texts is particularly well-handled, using the sister as a comic pressure valve.

Character consistency is strong. Rhys's "I like you. Actually like you. Not PR like. Real like" is perfectly calibrated to his profile—inarticulate when genuine, reaching for authenticity and landing somewhere endearingly clumsy. Cerys's oscillation between suspicion ("Bit like that though") and reluctant warmth is convincing. Mrs. Pritchard's "adequate at best" is a perfect deployment of her character.

The emotional beats are well-paced. The confrontation about whether Rhys knew photographers were following him is the chapter's strongest moment—it introduces genuine conflict with real stakes, and the resolution (wine on back steps, tentative reconnection) feels appropriately messy rather than tidy.

WEAKNESSES:

The chapter occasionally leans too heavily on the rapid-fire exchange format, which can feel like a tic. Some exchanges are so compressed they risk losing emotional weight—the confrontation about the photographers, which should be the chapter's most charged moment, resolves perhaps too quickly into banter. "It tasted like disappointment" is the chapter's one genuinely weak line—a cliché that doesn't fit the otherwise grounded prose style. Similarly, "something in his eyes that made her stomach flip" is a tell-don't-show moment in a chapter that otherwise shows admirably.

The phone-throwing-into-bins gag is funny but slightly strains credibility—it's a bit too theatrical even for Rhys. The "Nelson jacket" references throughout are slightly confusing without clearer establishment of what this costume actually is.

Overall, this is confident, accomplished work that executes its plan with skill. The dialogue is the clear standout—consistently funny, character-specific, and emotionally honest. Minor weaknesses in a few tell-don't-show moments and one or two clichés prevent it from being exceptional.
